          "parts": [
            "from pathlib import Path\r\n\r\ncontent = r\"\"\"# N02: LIGHT CRACKS THROUGH\r\n\r\n**Turning:** GLASS (G)  \r\n**Timeline:** A5-T2 through A5-T11 (primary on-page window)  \r\n**POV Roster:** Tiffani Merrow (Track 1) • Corlexi (Track 2) • Jhace Torrins (Track 3)  \r\n**Structure:** 12 chapters  \r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 1: MISSING\r\n\r\n*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T2*\r\n\r\nThe cot was empty.\r\n\r\nTiffani stopped in the doorway with her slate tight to her forearm and chalk ready. She had a three-Tide protocol built around him—breath cadence, tremor amplitude, pupil behavior under bright corridor light, autonomic drift, pain response, the full ladder of Stage Four markers before the body started compensating and the record got muddy.\r\n\r\nThe sheets weren’t thrown back.\r\n\r\nThey were folded. Corner to corner. Creased. Intentional.\r\n\r\nA man paralyzed from the sternum down did not fold sheets.\r\n\r\nShe walked in and did what she always did first: reduce the room to counts.\r\n\r\nStone floor: clean. No grit trail. No heel drag line. No scuff where a boot toe caught while hauling dead weight.\r\n\r\nCup on the side table—placed at arm’s reach from where his shoulder would have been. He couldn’t reach it yesterday. The calibration strap was missing. So was his secondary slate. Three copper-stained chalk pieces—kept separate because residue reads cleaner when you don’t contaminate it with common white.\r\n\r\nHis primary measurement slate remained.\r\n\r\nThe heavy one.\r\n\r\nThe one that mattered to anyone trying to control the narrative.\r\n\r\nShe wrote, neat and uniform:\r\n\r\n*Cot empty. Sheets folded. Cup repositioned. Tools missing: calibration strap, secondary slate, copper-stained chalk (3). Tools present: primary slate.*\r\n\r\nShe didn’t look at the cot while she wrote. Absence invited story. Story invited mistakes.\r\n\r\nFootsteps in the hall. A latch. Not a careful entry—just hesitant.\r\n\r\nA medical clerk edged into the doorway. Young. Trying to hold his jaw still and failing.\r\n\r\n“The director wants you. Records.”\r\n\r\n“After.” Tiffani didn’t turn. “Explain patient evacuation procedure.”\r\n\r\n“There wasn’t one.” His mouth tightened at one corner. “No authorization filed. No transfer order. No discharge.”\r\n\r\nTiffani’s chalk paused.\r\n\r\n“Then he’s still recorded as admitted.”\r\n\r\n“That’s… the problem.” The clerk swallowed. “Someone removed the admission ledger page. The director says protocol is unclear now.”\r\n\r\nUnclear meant instructed.\r\n\r\nTiffani wrote smaller, low on the slate where a casual confiscation wouldn’t notice:\r\n\r\n*Obstruction: administrative, active.*\r\n\r\nThe clerk left quickly, as if distance could become innocence.\r\n\r\nTiffani waited six heartbeats—enough time for panic to spread—to see if anyone came to stop her. No one did. That was its own kind of permission.\r\n\r\nShe went for the guard.\r\n\r\nHe stood outside the door, too straight, too new. Uniform still crisp at the seams.\r\n\r\n“Patient Jhace Torrins. Stage Four. Admitted A4-T19. Paralysis sternum down. No motor function. When did he leave?”\r\n\r\n“He didn’t.” The guard snapped straighter. “No one entered. No one left. I’ve been here since second bell.”\r\n\r\n“The door was watched continuously.”\r\n\r\n“Yes, ma’am.”\r\n\r\n“No breaks. No distractions. No messages that pulled your eyes off this frame.”\r\n\r\nHis gaze flicked down the corridor and back. Fingers tightened on the jamb. “Standard sweep. Maybe forty heartbeats.”\r\n\r\n“Forty.” Tiffani wrote it down.\r\n\r\nWhile the chalk moved, she watched him: shallow breath, pupils wide for bright corridor light, knuckles pale from grip.\r\n\r\nHe was either lying.\r\n\r\nOr he’d seen something and decided that naming it would get him punished.\r\n\r\nShe didn’t press. Pressure improved lies.\r\n\r\n“Thank you,” she said, and left him with his fear.\r\n\r\nInside, she shut the door and stood in the room where a paralyzed man had become an administrative inconvenience.\r\n\r\nThe grid reference-hum carried a second band in this wing. Low. Persistent. It sat under the normal vibration in the stone and didn’t behave like background drift. She’d noticed it yesterday and declined to log it because noticing wasn’t confirmation.\r\n\r\nCopper rose behind her tongue. Stronger than yesterday.\r\n\r\nHer fingers twitched once around the chalk.\r\n\r\nShe left that off the slate.\r\n\r\nIf he moved, there was a vector. If someone moved him, there was evidence. Rooms didn’t lie. People did. Institutions did. Stone didn’t.\r\n\r\nShe started at the threshold and worked clockwise.\r\n\r\nDoorframe: no fresh marks. Wall: no shoulder smear, no cloth rub. Floor: no drag line. Window: latched; dust on the sill undisturbed.\r\n\r\nThe ash bin by the cold stove held half-burned paper. She knelt and sifted with two fingers. Most of it collapsed to black powder. One strip held long enough to read:\r\n\r\n*…locked maintenance corridor…requires two hands + walking…impossible…*\r\n\r\nA report.\r\n\r\nSomeone wrote it. Someone burned it.\r\n\r\nShe slid the scrap into her notes.\r\n\r\nFootsteps again. Faster. Not careful.\r\n\r\nBram filled the doorway, coat rumpled, eyes already tracking the empty cot. His mouth tried to form humor and failed.\r\n\r\n“Great,” he said. “I love a room where reality quit.”\r\n\r\n“Not reality.” Tiffani stood, dusting ash off her fingertips onto her trousers. “Authority.”\r\n\r\nBram’s jaw worked. “Yesterday he couldn’t move his fingers.”\r\n\r\n“Correct.”\r\n\r\n“Today he’s gone.”\r\n\r\n“Correct.”\r\n\r\n“That’s not clerical.”\r\n\r\n“No.” Tiffani kept her voice level. Anger got remembered. Calm got underestimated. “Someone removed a ledger page and burned a report. That takes permission.”\r\n\r\nShe lifted the primary slate, tested the weight, ran her thumb along the worn edge where Jhace always rested his grip. No note. No instruction. Just placement.\r\n\r\nA deliberate choice: leave one object that could be seized and misread.\r\n\r\nShe set it back exactly where it had been.\r\n\r\nBram watched her. “So what now?”\r\n\r\n“Now I follow the physical trail.” She tapped her own slate once. “Residue. mode surges. stress behavior. He didn’t vanish. He moved or he was moved.”\r\n\r\n“And if he doesn’t want to be found?”\r\n\r\nShe didn’t answer. Want wasn’t measurable. Her methodology didn’t include it.\r\n\r\nShe packed. Slate. chalk. scrap. calipers. wedge gauge. Routine movements, precise and practiced. The buckles snapped shut.\r\n\r\nBram exhaled. “We’re going to end up in a file nobody can access.”\r\n\r\n“Then we make copies before they burn ours.”\r\n\r\nThey left the room together. The guard remained at his post. Tiffani didn’t look at him as she passed.\r\n\r\nSeven impossible holds in three Tides. Copper-gold residue where dispersal physics said it shouldn’t concentrate. A second band in the hum that behaved like a signature.\r\n\r\nSomeone was working without paying visible cost markers.\r\n\r\nSomeone was leaving proof.\r\n\r\nTiffani started down the stairs. Her right knee caught on the third step, locked, released. She adjusted her gait without pausing. Her hand trembled once against the rail.\r\n\r\nNeither went on the slate.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\nA bass strike hit the building.\r\n\r\nNot a crack. Not a ring. A low, muffled impact that came up through the stone, up through her boots, and into her molars. The stairwell flexed. Dust sifted from a seam overhead. Lamp chains began to swing.\r\n\r\nBram froze mid-step with a hand lifted, useless.\r\n\r\nTiffani held her breath and listened with her whole body.\r\n\r\nThe grid reference-hum stuttered. The second band swelled, dipped, then settled heavier.\r\n\r\n“Quiet,” she said, and meant: don’t move until you know what’s failing.\r\n\r\nFor four heartbeats the only sound was chain creak and distant shouting that hadn’t found a name for the new emergency.\r\n\r\nThen the shove came. Not impact—pressure. The stairwell door trembled in its frame. Hinges squealed.\r\n\r\nThe seam under the door darkened.\r\n\r\nWater forced through first as a line, then a spread, then a palm. Cold. Salt-sharp. Loaded with silt. The smell wasn’t clean sea—it was harbor rot and scraped bottom mud.\r\n\r\nBram backed up. “No. No—”\r\n\r\nThe door bucked. The frame split along an old crack that had just been given a reason. Water punched through in a sheet and slapped the stairwell wall hard enough to throw spray across stone.\r\n\r\nGlass shattered above them—one narrow corridor pane giving up. The next surge came through that opening and down the steps, grinding, carrying splinters, rope ends, and a plank spinning end-over-end.\r\n\r\nPeople screamed on the landing below. The sound cut off fast as water found them.\r\n\r\nTiffani grabbed Bram by the collar and hauled him up, away from the stairwell mouth, toward higher steps. Her kit slammed her hip with each movement. Brine chased their heels, licking each tread in quick cold pulses.\r\n\r\nSaint Merro had been built to keep the sea outside.\r\n\r\nThe Brinehammer didn’t care about design intent.\r\n\r\nIt was in the streets now. And everything that had been “stable” was about to get re-priced.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 2: FORM\r\n\r\n*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T2*\r\n\r\nCorlexi’s workspace served two lives.\r\n\r\nOn the table: grain tallies, transport costs, dull merchant arguments that made a man forget he was being watched.\r\n\r\nOn the walls—when uncovered—incident maps, cost progression curves, termination thresholds, name lists compiled without consent because consent slowed control.\r\n\r\nA knock came at the wrong rhythm.\r\n\r\nHe moved without haste. Charts slid under a ledger. Shoulders softened into merchant posture.\r\n\r\nAn older woman stood in the doorway. Textile calluses. Eyes that didn’t waste motion.\r\n\r\n“Message from Salee,” she said. “She says you’re documenting the Dregs.”\r\n\r\n“I document inventory,” Corlexi replied. “Documentation keeps business from bleeding.”\r\n\r\n“Salee says documentation *is* business.” The woman’s gaze stayed on his face. “She wanted you to know she sees you.”\r\n\r\nShe left.\r\n\r\nCorlexi waited until her footfall faded. Then he unbuttoned his shirt to the waist.\r\n\r\nScars cut diagonal across his torso—pale seams where copper-gold fused into tissue during the Piercing. Twelve fragments, each no larger than a thumbnail. Counted enough times to become reflex.\r\n\r\nHe tested.\r\n\r\nNo tremor. No exhaustion drift. No vein-lines creeping up his forearms.\r\n\r\nHe focused on a crooked lamp bracket across the room. Read it: iron. stress at the joint. fasteners failing by degrees for at least two Anneals.\r\n\r\nHe adjusted the structure.\r\n\r\nThe bracket settled with a soft click and held as if it had never been wrong.\r\n\r\nForm. Structure maintained.\r\n\r\nNo filament light. No copper spill. No visible cost marker.\r\n\r\nThat absence made him valuable.\r\n\r\nIt also made him an anomaly that would eventually be “resolved.”\r\n\r\nHe buttoned up and opened the collapse reports.\r\n\r\nSeven incidents in three Tides. Saint Merro held most of them—still bleeding, still pretending it had regained baseline. The holds were too clean. Casualty projections weren’t matching. Residue was concentrating in ways standard dispersal didn’t produce.\r\n\r\nSomeone else was operating outside the visible cost curve.\r\n\r\nHe wrote, not as speculation, but as risk management:\r\n\r\n*Internal shard integration may suppress standard cost markers. Confirm. Identify additional subjects.*\r\n\r\nA low vibration rolled through the wall—slow enough to register as a change, not background. He didn’t look at the window. Looking was a tell.\r\n\r\nThe corridor outside filled with directed foot traffic. Not panic. Routing. Networks.\r\n\r\nA second knock. Harder.\r\n\r\nHe opened the door to a runner with salt on his cuffs and a textile knot tied to his wrist.\r\n\r\n“Saint Merro got struck,” the runner said. “Water in the streets. People moving. Refugees coming.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi kept his face dull. “Why tell me.”\r\n\r\n“Because you write,” the runner said. “Writing turns into planning. Planning keeps floors from dropping.”\r\n\r\nA folded list went into Corlexi’s hand. Not names. Needs: rope, boards, dry cloth, nails, bread, wedges. Door bracing. Makeshift splinting. Clean water.\r\n\r\nHe felt, briefly, the registry become useful the way a prybar was useful. Not moral. Effective.\r\n\r\nHe slid the list under grain totals. “Where’s Salee.”\r\n\r\n“Routing the first wave. Also—your building’s taking load.”\r\n\r\n“Load.”\r\n\r\n“Bodies,” the runner snapped. “Too many. Too fast. Floors weren’t built for it.”\r\n\r\nRunner gone.\r\n\r\nCorlexi listened to his building. Stone carried weight clean. Wood carried it late. The disagreement had been there for months. Now it mattered.\r\n\r\nHe went downstairs.\r\n\r\nResidents were already packing. Not a formal shelter. Just a place people believed would still have a roof when patched structures elsewhere failed.\r\n\r\nA woman with brine-stiff hair shoved a bundle under a table. A man lifted a child onto a bench. Someone slammed a door; the frame answered with a complaint.\r\n\r\nCorlexi saw the central beam: hairline split at a join, old damage widened by sudden shifting load. It had an hour. Maybe two.\r\n\r\nIf it dropped, people would surge. Surge turned into uncontrolled coupling. Uncontrolled coupling turned into bodies.\r\n\r\nHe could intervene.\r\n\r\nHe could be seen.\r\n\r\nSalee’s people were here. He recognized them: eyes tracking exits, hands brushing beams without looking like they were measuring.\r\n\r\nHe moved like a merchant checking stock. Set down a crate, leaned, and slid focus into the split join.\r\n\r\nForm. Quiet. Precise.\r\n\r\nHe didn’t “fix” it. He redistributed load enough to buy time. The split stopped widening. Vibration steadied.\r\n\r\nCost: none visible.\r\n\r\nRisk: immediate.\r\n\r\nA man near the wall turned his head sharply, as if he’d felt something without knowing what. The runner’s eyes flicked to Corlexi’s hands.\r\n\r\nCorlexi straightened, picked up a sack of grain, and performed irritation.\r\n\r\n“If you’re stacking people, stack them against stone, not mid-span. You want the floor to stay a floor.”\r\n\r\nIt sounded like merchant complaint.\r\n\r\nIt was also correct.\r\n\r\nPeople shifted. Load redistributed by human choice. That mattered. It gave his adjustment a mundane cause.\r\n\r\nUpstairs, he wrote one line on a merchant sheet:\r\n\r\n*Brinehammer displacement begun. Dregs taking load. Structural risk increased. Cover risk increased.*\r\n\r\nThen, smaller:\r\n\r\n*Salee sees me. Now her network may have felt me.*\r\n\r\nHe folded the page under grain totals and resumed the posture of a man who cared only about prices.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 3: WAKE\r\n\r\n*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T3 through A5-T7*\r\n\r\nSeven reports. Seven incidents. Three Tides.\r\n\r\nTiffani laid them across her workspace and refused long blinks. Patterns showed themselves when you didn’t flinch.\r\n\r\nSeam collapse near Junction Twelve boundary. Estimated hold duration: two hundred heartbeats. Zero casualties where her structural projection demanded four minimum. Copper-gold residue concentrated at load junctions instead of dispersing into surrounding material.\r\n\r\nFoundation stabilization at Merchant Row—same residue behavior.\r\n\r\nBridge support failure at the southern canal—again.\r\n\r\nEach site carried a mode surge in the grid reference-hum that didn’t match ambient drift. Not noise. A signature.\r\n\r\nShe plotted locations on a city map and drew lines with calibrated tools. The geometry held.\r\n\r\nThe vector pointed northeast.\r\n\r\nGrindheim.\r\n\r\nA quiet scrape at her door. Not a knock. Someone trying not to exist in the corridor’s memory.\r\n\r\nCorran stepped in and shut it behind him. Mid-level Guild administration. Tired eyes. Restricted stamp on the folder in his hand. A man walking into a decision he already hated.\r\n\r\n“Unofficially,” he said, “you did not get this from me.”\r\n\r\nHe set the folder down. Didn’t sit.\r\n\r\n“The director is calling it clerical loss,” Corran continued. “Lost ledger pages. Misfiled routing. Your search authorization gets revoked next Tide.”\r\n\r\nTiffani moved on instinct to the archive cabinet. Pulled the Saint Merro incident drawer.\r\n\r\nIt didn’t open.\r\n\r\nNot stuck—locked. Fresh metal. New keyway.\r\n\r\nCorran stared at the floor.\r\n\r\n“They moved fast,” Tiffani said.\r\n\r\n“They moved before I could stop them,” Corran replied.\r\n\r\nShe rested her fingers on the new lock long enough to feel its cold, then stepped back.\r\n\r\n“Good,” she said. “Now it’s measurable.”\r\n\r\nCorran’s throat worked. “Someone doesn’t want you following this.”\r\n\r\nShe opened the folder.\r\n\r\nEighth incident. Grindheim Dregs. Old textile district. Same residue concentration. Same hold cleanliness. Same missing casualties.\r\n\r\n“Why help me,” she asked.\r\n\r\n“Because your ledger page wasn’t lost,” Corran said, low. “It was removed. I saw the request in archive before it got scrubbed. Administrative channel. Crown-level reach.”\r\n\r\nObstruction: confirmed.\r\n\r\nInterference: funded.\r\n\r\nTiffani kept her face still. “What do you want.”\r\n\r\n“When you understand what happened—when you learn what was done to him—send word outside the official channels.” Corran didn’t offer method. “Don’t send it where they can erase it.”\r\n\r\nHe left without waiting for agreement.\r\n\r\nTwo Tides later, she found Bram in the Guild hall with a chipped cup and something dark inside it.\r\n\r\n“I need access to Grindheim,” she said. “Before revocation lands. You have textile ties and harbor adjacency. Get me a reason that opens doors.”\r\n\r\nBram looked up like his mouth wanted humor and his eyes refused it. “I’m not a spy.”\r\n\r\n“You can move where I can’t,” she said. “And people talk when they don’t feel threatened.”\r\n\r\nHe exhaled once. “That is… unfortunately true.”\r\n\r\n“Get me in.”\r\n\r\nHis cup hit the table hard enough to rattle the saucer. “When this goes bad, I’m haunting you. I’ll move your chalk. I’ll swap your measurements. I’ll make your lines crooked.”\r\n\r\nTiffani didn’t look up. “Accepted.”\r\n\r\nShe returned to her workspace and finished the map.\r\n\r\nEight incidents. Then nine, after another report arrived through hands that avoided stamps and signatures. The vector held: Grindheim’s Dregs. A place where buildings leaned because standing alone was expensive, and information moved through people instead of paperwork.\r\n\r\nAs she drew the final line, copper surged behind her tongue.\r\n\r\nHer right knee locked mid-motion, released, then refused to cooperate for six heartbeats. She gripped the desk until it passed.\r\n\r\nShe wrote neither fact down.\r\n\r\nIf she logged it, she’d be labeled compromised. If she was labeled compromised, she’d be removed from the work. If she was removed from the work, he stayed missing.\r\n\r\nShe packed her kit. Slate. Chalk. The burned scrap. The off-channel reports.\r\n\r\nTomorrow they left for Grindheim.\r\n\r\nTomorrow she followed a physics trail into territory where institutions had no grip and networks had all of it.\r\n\r\nAt the bottom of the stairs outside her workspace, she wrote one line in a private margin:\r\n\r\n*Authority chose concealment. Physics will decide who’s lying.*\r\n\r\nHer hand shook while she wrote it.\r\n\r\nShe still didn’t move the tremor into the official log.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 4: SEARCHING\r\n\r\n*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T3*\r\n\r\nHe woke expecting paralysis.\r\n\r\nHis fingers moved.\r\n\r\nJhace stayed still and ran the test the way he ran every inspection: smallest system first, then expand.\r\n\r\nRight hand: functional. Delay of three heartbeats between intention and motion.  \r\nLeft hand: functional. Same delay.  \r\nToes: responsive, but late—as if signals had been rerouted through bad junctions.  \r\nLegs: sensation present. Not memory. Present.\r\n\r\nThe tremor was gone.\r\n\r\nHe raised his hands into thin light. Steady. No flutter at the wrist. No shake in the knuckles.\r\n\r\nRelief didn’t arrive.\r\n\r\nHeat sat behind his sternum instead. Constant pressure, not pain. Too tight to ignore, too steady to call “temporary.”\r\n\r\nHe sat up.\r\n\r\nThe room tilted, corrected a heartbeat late. His body lagged behind command. He didn’t fight it. Fighting made mistakes.\r\n\r\nNot the infirmary.\r\n\r\nA narrow maintenance room. Cold pipe run on one wall. Low steel table bolted to the floor. Dust in corners. Chalk scuffs on stone.\r\n\r\nHis secondary slate lay on the table. Calibration strap beside it. Three copper-stained chalk pieces set in a line, tips aligned.\r\n\r\nNot dumped. Placed.\r\n\r\nHis primary slate was missing.\r\n\r\nHe remembered leaving it behind—the heavy one, the one that could become evidence in the wrong hands. He’d left it where “admission” could be altered and blamed.\r\n\r\nHe looked at his palm.\r\n\r\nCopper-gold light sat under the skin in thin threads that weren’t veins and weren’t ink. Flex: brighter. Still: dimmer. Never gone.\r\n\r\nMemory came in strips.\r\n\r\nThe cot. No voice. No movement. Breathing and waiting.\r\n\r\nA figure in the doorway.\r\n\r\nNot shadowed. Not lit. Light behaved wrong around it.\r\n\r\nA burn in his palm.\r\n\r\nThe sensation of being mapped—nerve, vessel, joint—surveyed and set.\r\n\r\nNo face. No voice. No reason.\r\n\r\nOnly certainty: it wasn’t rescue.\r\n\r\nIt was procedure.\r\n\r\nA low vibration rolled through the structure. Heavy enough to make the pipe run above him tremble.\r\n\r\nThen the impact—deep, muffled, clean through stone.\r\n\r\nPressure followed. The steel table complained against its bolts. Dust fell from a ceiling seam.\r\n\r\nHe stood too fast. Lag punished him; his legs accepted the command late and he caught himself on the pipe.\r\n\r\nHeat surged behind his sternum. Copper flooded his mouth.\r\n\r\nBrine rode in it.\r\n\r\nHe strapped his kit on by muscle memory. Buckles clicked. Strap bit his shoulder. Good. Real.\r\n\r\nHe wrote one line on the secondary slate:\r\n\r\n*A5-T3. Mobility restored. Tremor absent. Phase lag present. Core pressure constant.*\r\n\r\nHe tested coupling.\r\n\r\nFocused on a pipe bracket.\r\n\r\nThe coupling didn’t start in his hands.\r\n\r\nIt started behind his sternum. Tight bloom, outward push. Light bled through ribs into metal and wall. He felt stress in the joint without touching it.\r\n\r\nHe pushed.\r\n\r\nThe bracket settled. Pipe vibration smoothed. Structure complied.\r\n\r\nHeat hit hard enough to soften his vision at the edges.\r\n\r\nHe broke coupling immediately.\r\n\r\nCost: heat. Visual edge loss. Phase lag worsening post-hold.\r\n\r\nHe wrote:\r\n\r\n*Coupling origin shifted (sternum/core). Cost markers: heat surge + peripheral blur + phase lag worsens after hold.*\r\n\r\nAbove him, water struck stone. Then glass gave up—one pane, then another—followed by shouting that cut short too quickly.\r\n\r\nMobel surfaced in his mind like a tool finally remembered.\r\n\r\nThe other shard-bearer. The one who held without visible cost markers.\r\n\r\nIf anyone had language for integration, it was Mobel.\r\n\r\nJhace took what he needed and left through the maintenance corridor named in a burned report—locked, requiring two hands and walking.\r\n\r\nHe had both hands.\r\n\r\nHe could walk.\r\n\r\nThat was enough.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 5: AUTHORITY\r\n\r\n*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T3*\r\n\r\nIn the Dregs, news arrived as pressure before it arrived as words.\r\n\r\nCorlexi was recalculating a termination curve when the building’s vibration changed—low shove under the normal band. Stone transmitted it clean. Wood transmitted it late.\r\n\r\nA boy with a textile knot on his wrist arrived ten minutes after.\r\n\r\n“Saint Merro got struck,” the boy said.\r\n\r\n“With what,” Corlexi asked.\r\n\r\nThe boy’s throat worked. “Sea came into streets. Doors blew. Glass blew. People got dragged like salvage.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi set his pencil down. Kept his face neutral.\r\n\r\n“How many,” he asked.\r\n\r\n“Enough,” the boy said, then took a coin and vanished.\r\n\r\nDisaster did that. It turned “later” into “now.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi watched the Dregs shift. Bundles moved. Boards carried. Rope dragged. Salee’s network was visible in the routing—fast, quiet, efficient.\r\n\r\nThen the second wave arrived.\r\n\r\nA Crown-marked wagon rolled into the district with a canvas banner and a soup pot strapped to the back. Two clerks with clean cuffs followed. Guards followed them, posture rigid, hands near batons.\r\n\r\nThe clerks set a folding table in the street and laid down blank pages like they were claiming territory.\r\n\r\n“Displaced residents,” the lead clerk called. “Relief allocation requires identification. Name and district. Manifestation status if known. Injuries noted. Supplies issued.”\r\n\r\nPeople watched from doorways. Nobody cheered.\r\n\r\nSoup in exchange for a registry.\r\n\r\nCorlexi wrote:\r\n\r\n*A5-T3. Brinehammer displacement event. Secondary effects: uncontrolled coupling spikes + increased Crown leverage to implement registry via relief.*\r\n\r\nA manifestor in line tried to hold a buckling awning while keeping a child from being shoved underfoot. Tremor in both arms. Stage Two pushing into Three. Her hold broke. The frame snapped and clipped a man’s shoulder.\r\n\r\nCrowd surged.\r\n\r\nTwo hands lit with copper-gold filaments—instinct coupling. Light jumped, stuttered, then held long enough for bodies to clear. Both manifestors recoiled, pain in their faces, and one retched brine onto the street.\r\n\r\nA guard stepped toward them.\r\n\r\nSalee’s network moved faster.\r\n\r\nSomeone shouted “Brace left!” and a post went into place. The crisis shifted away from the clerks without becoming a neat incident on their pages.\r\n\r\nBefore noon, Corlexi received a sealed note.\r\n\r\nCrown wax. Simple. Heavy.\r\n\r\nHe read:\r\n\r\n**Directive:** accelerate compilation. Prioritize high-output manifestors. Prepare classification categories for anomalous cost suppression and non-consensual stabilization events. Assign liaisons to relief points.\r\n\r\nRelief as capture.\r\n\r\nHe folded it under invoices and wrote where no one would look twice:\r\n\r\n*Acceleration acknowledged. Cover risk increased. Salee aware. Relief liaison will tighten scrutiny.*\r\n\r\nIf Saint Merro’s ledgers were burning, the Crown would draft new ones using brine as ink and call it safety.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 6: DREGS\r\n\r\n*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T5*\r\n\r\nSaint Merro didn’t recover. It redistributed damage.\r\n\r\nTwo Tides after the Brinehammer, the lower streets still carried salt rot and smashed kelp. Harbor quarter doors were gone. Window frames were jagged. Boards were jammed into cracks where water had forced the masonry open. Every “temporary” brace made the next failure more expensive.\r\n\r\nThe infirmary still stood.\r\n\r\nStanding wasn’t the same as functioning.\r\n\r\nTiffani walked past triage cots lined in the corridor where Jhace had vanished. People lay wrapped in damp blankets. Lips cracked by brine. Eyes refusing sleep. One manifestor shook so hard a healer had to pin his shoulders and speak low until his breathing slowed.\r\n\r\nThe records door remained sealed. Fresh wax over old wax.\r\n\r\nAuthority didn’t rebuild. It layered.\r\n\r\nBram waited for her at the side exit.\r\n\r\n“You’re going to pretend this didn’t change everything,” he said.\r\n\r\n“It changed conditions,” Tiffani replied. “Not method.”\r\n\r\nBram followed. “That’s what people say right before they die to prove they’re consistent.”\r\n\r\nTiffani didn’t give him the satisfaction of reacting. “I’m still correct.”\r\n\r\nShe spread the reports again. The Brinehammer added failures. Failures added residue. Residue added trackable signatures.\r\n\r\nThe clean-hold pattern still pointed northeast.\r\n\r\nGrindheim.\r\n\r\nTravel was restricted now. Stabilization protocol. Crown co-mark stamped onto Guild authority. Her search permissions suspended.\r\n\r\nCorran didn’t hand her an apology. He handed her a textile shipment escort pass with Bram’s name on it and kept his eyes off the corridor cameras.\r\n\r\nThat night they left with a wagon crew hauling salvage cloth and salt-stiff rope toward Grindheim. Detours around undermined stone. Temporary spans held by manifestors whose hands shook too hard to hold their own cups. One young woman coughed brine and kept coupling anyway because the bridge still needed to stay a bridge.\r\n\r\nBy the time Grindheim’s Dregs rose into view, Tiffani’s knee had locked and released enough times that she started counting intervals.\r\n\r\nShe still didn’t log it.\r\n\r\nThey found Salee by following motion.\r\n\r\nInside an unsignposted building, Salee stood over a routing surface—chalk marks, string loops, and a glass shard positioned to throw light sideways without showing a flame.\r\n\r\n“You’re the methodist,” Salee said.\r\n\r\n“That’s a label,” Tiffani replied.\r\n\r\nSalee’s eyes moved over Tiffani’s kit. “Labels tell me where to place you.”\r\n\r\n“I’m looking for Jhace Torrins,” Tiffani said. “He vanished under institutional suppression. His trail points here.”\r\n\r\nSalee didn’t feign surprise. “Everything points here when the world breaks. Saint Merro got punched. Refugees follow. Authority follows refugees. You walked in carrying order like it’s a shield.”\r\n\r\n“Order keeps bodies alive,” Tiffani said.\r\n\r\n“Bodies stay alive by adapting,” Salee said. “Order shows up after the counting stops.”\r\n\r\n“You want my network,” Salee continued. “Witnesses. Routing. Safe passage. The Dregs doesn’t give that away.”\r\n\r\n“What do you want,” Tiffani asked.\r\n\r\nSalee tapped a copper-stained chalk piece on the table. “Your protocols. How you map cost. How you predict the next failure before it kills the operator. My people hold without permission and pay alone. I want a way to see the payment coming.”\r\n\r\n“Mutual utility,” Tiffani said.\r\n\r\n“Terms,” Salee replied. “No faces in your notes. No names unless offered. No routing handed to authority.”\r\n\r\n“And you give me access,” Tiffani said.\r\n\r\nSalee nodded once. “Truth as we see it. It comes dirty.”\r\n\r\n“Accepted.”\r\n\r\nA runner burst in. “More Saint Merro arrivals. Water took the kiln quarter.”\r\n\r\nBram went pale.\r\n\r\nTiffani’s throat tightened. The Brinehammer was still charging them. It wasn’t done writing.\r\n\r\nShe opened her slate.\r\n\r\n“Then we document faster,” she said, because she didn’t have a better weapon that wouldn’t break her own rules.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 7: TRACKING\r\n\r\n*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T6*\r\n\r\nThe coast smelled wrong—brine, rot, and silt that belonged under stone, not on top of it.\r\n\r\nJhace walked north with his kit tight against his ribs. Phase lag made every step arrive late. He corrected by counting and refusing to rush.\r\n\r\nMobel’s wake stayed readable: dock pilings braced too clean, warehouse walls stabilized mid-fail with residue concentrated at joints, stone stairs corrected without the scuff patterns tremoring hands left behind.\r\n\r\nThe Brinehammer made stress points everywhere.\r\n\r\nThat made the shard behind his sternum restless.\r\n\r\nHe refused three holds before noon and paid for refusal anyway. Heat built. Phase lag worsened. His hands stayed steady while the rest of him felt less aligned.\r\n\r\nAt a coastal settlement, a pier crane leaned toward collapse. A soaked man stood under it and stared up without moving.\r\n\r\nJhace walked past.\r\n\r\nTen steps later the shard surged without permission.\r\n\r\nLight pressed out through his ribs into stone. The crane joint locked. The lean stopped.\r\n\r\nJhace cut coupling immediately and kept moving.\r\n\r\nHeat punched his chest. Copper flooded his mouth. When he wiped his wrist, a thin smear of blood came away. Not a wound—internal spill.\r\n\r\nHis palm glowed brighter. Copper-gold threads climbed another finger’s width toward his wrist.\r\n\r\nHe wrote while walking:\r\n\r\n*A5-T6. Involuntary hold triggered by proximity + saturation stress. Cost: heat spike + oral bleed + spread accelerated.*\r\n\r\nWitnesses spoke about “the calm one” who fixed a brace and didn’t shake afterward. Mobel. Two or three Tides ahead, depending on who remembered truth and who remembered what kept them safe.\r\n\r\nRefugees moved inland along the road—carts rattling, bundles wet, children carried too high to keep them out of water. One woman saw the glow under Jhace’s sleeve and backed away like he was an exposed edge.\r\n\r\nJhace pulled his sleeve down and kept moving.\r\n\r\nMobel’s wake led toward higher ground—fresh residue at a culvert, clean joint locks on a bridge support undermined by floodwater.\r\n\r\nJhace followed.\r\n\r\nThe pressure behind his sternum didn’t ease.\r\n\r\nIf anything, it felt more certain.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 8: ENTROPY\r\n\r\n*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T7*\r\n\r\nVelocity-Heights sat above the Dregs on newer stone and older arrogance.\r\n\r\nIt didn’t take the Brinehammer as water in streets. It took it as bodies at gates.\r\n\r\nRefugees were being funneled into glass-roofed halls and “temporary” shelters built from lattice and pane. The architecture looked precise. The loads were not. Wet bundles, too many feet, panic weight shifting without rhythm.\r\n\r\nCorlexi arrived alone under cover: merchant verification, relief routing assessment.\r\n\r\nReal purpose: Eries.\r\n\r\nHe found her in a public garden where glass lattice cut sunlight into hard lines across stone. Students sat around her with open slates.\r\n\r\n“The Brinehammer isn’t a moral event,” Eries said. “It isn’t punishment. It’s force meeting structure. If your structure lies to itself, force corrects the lie.”\r\n\r\nWhen the students dispersed, Eries didn’t turn to Corlexi immediately.\r\n\r\n“You’re not here for shipping,” she said.\r\n\r\n“Crown,” she added, and didn’t wait for an answer.\r\n\r\n“You write about decay,” Corlexi said.\r\n\r\n“I write about reality,” Eries replied. “People want blame because blame feels like control.”\r\n\r\n“The registry prevents uncontrolled risk.”\r\n\r\n“The registry labels risk,” Eries said. “After it’s already moving.”\r\n\r\nA shout rose beyond the garden wall. A relief crew dragged glass panes toward a shelter hall. The panes were strapped wrong. The load shifted. The top sheet flexed and began to bow.\r\n\r\nA crack snapped through the air and the sheet started to split.\r\n\r\nPeople scattered. Some didn’t move fast enough.\r\n\r\nEries stepped into the churn and said one word—“Down”—and the nearest students dropped without argument.\r\n\r\nCorlexi read the stress line instantly. The glass wasn’t annealed for this transport. It was built to sit, not be hauled through panic.\r\n\r\nHe could correct it with Form.\r\n\r\nHe also knew Crown guards were watching.\r\n\r\nHe moved anyway.\r\n\r\nHands on a wooden brace like any other man steadying a load. Under that physical cover, he adjusted strap path and brace angle with Form—small correction, clean redistribution.\r\n\r\nThe glass stopped singing. The split halted as a hairline.\r\n\r\nA guard stared directly at him.\r\n\r\nEries watched too, not surprised—confirming.\r\n\r\nWhen the crisis passed, Eries returned to the path and wiped glass dust from her palm.\r\n\r\n“You intervened,” she said.\r\n\r\n“I prevented waste.”\r\n\r\n“You prevented blood,” Eries corrected. “Without visible cost markers.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi kept his voice flat. “Observation is a habit.”\r\n\r\n“Reality is,” Eries said. “Your registry won’t stop holds from failing. It will decide who gets blamed when they do.”\r\n\r\n“What’s the alternative.”\r\n\r\n“Admit you don’t control failure,” Eries said. “You manage it. You choose where it lands. If you pretend you aren’t choosing, you still choose—blind.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi left the Heights with cover intact and a new problem: Eries now had proof of what he could do.\r\n\r\nKnowledge spread on its own schedule.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 9: COLD\r\n\r\n*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T8*\r\n\r\nThe Dregs didn’t keep trails the way stone kept scuffs.\r\n\r\nSalee’s network gave Tiffani what it could: three sightings in two Tides. A man moving wrong—step landing late, body correcting after. Hands steady when they shouldn’t be. A copper-gold glow glimpsed at a sleeve edge. Always northeast.\r\n\r\nTiffani mapped each point and watched the geometry degrade.\r\n\r\nNot because the pattern was false.\r\n\r\nBecause the pattern stopped being physical.\r\n\r\nIt became behavior.\r\n\r\nAt the Dregs edge, reliable sighting dissolved into “maybe” and “don’t ask again.”\r\n\r\nBram stood beside her under a bent awning. “He’s not here.”\r\n\r\n“The data doesn’t support that.”\r\n\r\n“The data doesn’t support anything,” Bram snapped, then softened. “People survive by lying here.”\r\n\r\nSalee approached without announcing herself. “You’ve reached the end of what physics can prove,” she said.\r\n\r\n“Physics doesn’t end.”\r\n\r\n“Your access ends,” Salee replied. “Your assumptions end.”\r\n\r\nTiffani kept her chalk steady. “He chose concealment.”\r\n\r\n“Or he chose not to be your experiment,” Salee said.\r\n\r\nBram watched Tiffani’s hands. “If he doesn’t want to be found, you’re tracking a decision.”\r\n\r\nTiffani wrote:\r\n\r\n*Trail cold. Physical markers absent. Variable introduced: will.*\r\n\r\nThe line felt like taking damage.\r\n\r\nThen she wrote:\r\n\r\n*Redirect objective: Mobel.*\r\n\r\nSalee nodded once. “That’s measurable.”\r\n\r\nBram’s voice dropped. “You still aren’t logging your own cost.”\r\n\r\nTiffani’s chalk snapped. She stared at the broken tip and put it away.\r\n\r\n“I’m fine.”\r\n\r\n“You’re lying,” Bram said. “I can see it.”\r\n\r\nTiffani didn’t answer.\r\n\r\nThat night she wrote one sentence in a private margin:\r\n\r\n*My body is becoming data I refuse to record.*\r\n\r\nHer hand shook as she wrote it.\r\n\r\nShe still didn’t move it into the official log.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 10: INTERSECTION\r\n\r\n*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T9*\r\n\r\nThe settlement had a lighthouse stump and a main street lined with broken glass left by the Brinehammer’s edge.\r\n\r\nJhace arrived and felt stress before he saw it: a warehouse frame pulled out of square, wet weight in the roof, one support joint already eating itself under shifting load.\r\n\r\nThe shard tightened.\r\n\r\nHe told it no.\r\n\r\nIt surged anyway.\r\n\r\nLight pressed outward through him and into stone. The frame settled. The joint locked long enough for people inside to get out.\r\n\r\nJhace cut coupling and took one step wrong as phase lag hit.\r\n\r\nHeat slammed his ribs. Copper flooded his mouth. Blood smeared thin on his wrist when he wiped. He didn’t stop to inspect it. Stopping made you catchable.\r\n\r\n“You’re the other one.”\r\n\r\nJhace turned.\r\n\r\nA woman in practical clothes stood in a broken doorway’s lee. No tremor. A tool on her belt—glass, wire, shard sliver—hummed faintly.\r\n\r\n“Define ‘other,’” Jhace said.\r\n\r\n“Internal integration,” she said. “Like Mobel.” She stepped closer. “Vael. I repair what can be repaired. I watch what can’t.”\r\n\r\n“You’re watching me.”\r\n\r\n“I’m measuring your wake,” Vael said. “You leave clean locks half the time without meaning to.”\r\n\r\n“I need answers. Mobel will have them.”\r\n\r\n“Mobel’s been running for three Anneals,” Vael said. “He doesn’t share. Being a solution makes you property.”\r\n\r\n“I’m being hunted,” Jhace said.\r\n\r\nVael nodded once. “Yes.”\r\n\r\n“How close.”\r\n\r\n“Two Tides behind. Maybe less now.” Vael’s eyes stayed steady. “They tidy after you. They correct residue. They ask questions after you leave and make people forget they answered.”\r\n\r\nJhace wrote fast:\r\n\r\n*A5-T9. Vael confirmed pursuit. Pursuer performs residue correction + witness management. External shard-tool exists; costs differ, not absent.*\r\n\r\n“What do you want,” he asked.\r\n\r\n“I want you to decide if catching Mobel is worth being caught,” Vael said. “The Brinehammer turned the coast into a field of failure points. Your shard can’t shut up. You’re trackable.”\r\n\r\nShe stepped back toward shadow.\r\n\r\n“Why tell me.”\r\n\r\nVael’s jaw tightened. “Because I’ve seen what happens when someone becomes infrastructure.”\r\n\r\nThen she was gone.\r\n\r\nJhace stood with copper in his mouth and pressure in his chest and road ahead leading toward Mobel.\r\n\r\nHe chose forward.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 11: COERCION\r\n\r\n*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T10*\r\n\r\nCorlexi returned to his workspace and knew it had been read.\r\n\r\nDust patterns were wrong. Ledger edge shifted. One pin rotated slightly—small, but deliberate.\r\n\r\nUnder his pillow: a copper-stained chalk piece with a maker notch he didn’t use.\r\n\r\nVael.\r\n\r\nA message without ink: *I can enter your life whenever I want.*\r\n\r\nHe pocketed it and left.\r\n\r\nThe Dregs had changed again. Refugees arrived steady—salt-cracked skin, damp bundles, eyes scanning upward for beams that might decide to fail.\r\n\r\nThe Crown relief table had grown. Two clerks wrote faster. A guard stood closer. Another guard stood behind, watching faces instead of hands.\r\n\r\n“Name and district,” the clerk called.\r\n\r\nA starving man gave a name and got soup.\r\n\r\nThe guard leaned in after, voice low and practiced: “Who manifested during the inundation. Who held. Who glowed.”\r\n\r\nThat wasn’t relief.\r\n\r\nThat was harvesting.\r\n\r\nCorlexi tracked the flow and saw Salee’s counter-flow—quiet diversions, redirected lines, people moved out of the capture funnel without making it obvious. The Dregs didn’t fight head-on. It rerouted.\r\n\r\nVael stood near a broken fountain, watching the street like it was a load diagram. Her shard-tool hummed faintly under cloth.\r\n\r\n“You searched my workspace,” Corlexi said when he reached her.\r\n\r\n“I confirmed what you are,” Vael replied. “Embedded Crown. Registry architect.”\r\n\r\n“And you,” Corlexi said, “are an independent operator with external coupling tech.”\r\n\r\n“Accurate.”\r\n\r\n“You could expose me.”\r\n\r\n“I could.” Vael didn’t smile. “Exposure isn’t my goal.”\r\n\r\n“State your goal.”\r\n\r\nVael’s eyes flicked to the relief table. “That table will turn into a net. They’ll call it help. They’ll call your paperwork ‘safety.’ Then they’ll start deciding who gets to live near stable stone and who gets pushed back into rot.”\r\n\r\n“You’re describing inevitability,” Corlexi said.\r\n\r\n“I’m describing what your Crown does when it panics,” Vael said. “And the Brinehammer made them panic.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi kept his posture calm. “So why contact me.”\r\n\r\n“Because you’re useful,” Vael said. “You see patterns. You measure costs. You understand thresholds. And your shards don’t make you pay in obvious ways.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi’s voice stayed flat. “You want access to my charts.”\r\n\r\n“I want leverage,” Vael corrected. “If they come for me, I want proof of what you’ve been building. If they come for the Dregs, I want your termination logic on record. Not in Crown archives. Somewhere that survives.”\r\n\r\n“You’re asking me to sabotage my directive.”\r\n\r\n“I’m asking you to plan for when your directive gets used as a blade,” Vael said. “Because you’re already writing categories for ‘anomalous cost suppression.’ You’re already drawing a box to put people in.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi watched the relief line. A woman with tremoring hands stood three places from the clerk, brine still crusted on her hair. Her child clung to her waist. The clerk’s pen hovered, waiting to turn a human into a row.\r\n\r\n“What do you know about Mobel,” Corlexi asked, quietly.\r\n\r\nVael’s gaze sharpened. “Enough to know you shouldn’t say his name in open air.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi didn’t react. “And the other anomaly. Clean holds without visible cost.”\r\n\r\nVael’s tool gave a faint hum, like glass under strain. “Cost doesn’t vanish. It changes address. If you haven’t learned where it’s going, you’re building blind.”\r\n\r\nEries’s words echoed without being present: *where does the cost go.*\r\n\r\nCorlexi looked back at Vael. “What are you offering.”\r\n\r\n“A trade,” Vael said. “You give me copies—select pages, not your whole spine. I keep your cover intact in the Dregs. And I tell you one thing your Crown doesn’t have yet.”\r\n\r\n“Which is.”\r\n\r\nVael leaned in just enough to keep her words from traveling. “Someone is cleaning wakes. Not yours. Not mine. Jhace’s. Mobel’s. They move behind the trail and make witnesses uncertain. They scrub residue. They are not Crown clerks.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi’s pulse stayed steady by practice. “How do you know.”\r\n\r\n“Because I do some of it myself,” Vael said. “And whoever’s behind them is better.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi stared at the relief table. The guard leaned in again. Pen moved. A name became a hook.\r\n\r\n“Your cover is already strained,” Vael said. “Salee sees you. The Brinehammer gave you excuses to intervene. Every excuse is a footprint.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi made a decision he didn’t like.\r\n\r\n“Tonight,” he said. “I’ll prepare copies.”\r\n\r\nVael nodded once. “Smart.”\r\n\r\n“And if I refuse.”\r\n\r\n“Then the Crown takes your notes anyway,” Vael said. “And you get to pretend you were surprised.”\r\n\r\nCorlexi didn’t answer.\r\n\r\nHe walked away with the chalk piece in his pocket and the ugly certainty that the Brinehammer hadn’t just flooded streets.\r\n\r\nIt had flooded systems.\r\n\r\nAnd systems, when flooded, showed you exactly where they were cracked.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## CHAPTER 12: PRESSURE\r\n\r\n*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T11*\r\n\r\nAfter the Brinehammer, language got sharp.\r\n\r\nPeople stopped saying *damage* when they meant *missing*. Stopped saying *help* when they meant *trade*. Stopped saying *stable* unless they’d just touched the beam and felt it agree.\r\n\r\nSalee’s building stayed unmarked from the street. Inside, it had grown teeth—more string lines, more chalk loops, more bodies moving through tight decisions without asking permission from a stamp.\r\n\r\nBram stood at the edge of the routing surface and watched a runner point, then point again, then shove a wedge into a woman’s hand like it was a ration. The woman nodded and moved. No committee. No paperwork. No “please.”\r\n\r\nIt was brutal.\r\n\r\nIt worked.\r\n\r\nTiffani adjusted her kit strap and waited until Salee’s eyes came up.\r\n\r\n“You’re late,” Salee said.\r\n\r\n“I was verifying,” Tiffani replied.\r\n\r\n“Verifying what.”\r\n\r\nTiffani put her slate down. “The last three sightings. All of them. Including the ones your runners were told to stop repeating.”\r\n\r\nSalee didn’t smile. “You want Mobel.”\r\n\r\n“I want the convergence,” Tiffani said. “Mobel is the only known operator who holds clean without the public cost markers. He’s the fastest way to understand what happened to Jhace.”\r\n\r\nBram made a small sound—half warning, half fatigue.\r\n\r\nSalee tapped the table twice. A runner stepped forward and laid down a strip of textile with four knots tied into it. No words. Just timing, direction, and the kind of caution you only see after people get punished for talking.\r\n\r\nTiffani read it, transferred it to her map, and felt the vector tighten again.\r\n\r\nNortheast. Always northeast.\r\n\r\nThe trail hadn’t died.\r\n\r\nIt had learned to be thin.\r\n\r\n“Your city is closing,” Salee said. “Not with locks. With movement.”\r\n\r\n“They don’t have the bodies to seal everything,” Bram said.\r\n\r\nSalee looked at him like he’d offered prayer as engineering. “They don’t need everything. They need chokepoints. They need fear. They need a reason to stop a cart and open a kit.”\r\n\r\nTiffani wrote:\r\n\r\n*Constraint shift: movement is now regulated variable. Expect witness degradation. Expect residue suppression.*\r\n\r\nSalee watched her chalk. “You’re still trying to turn will into physics.”\r\n\r\n“I’m trying to remove it as a factor,” Tiffani said.\r\n\r\n“You can’t remove it,” Salee replied. “You can only pretend you did.”\r\n\r\nA runner burst in—wet cuffs, brine ground into the fabric.\r\n\r\n“Relief table moved two streets closer,” the runner said. “They’re marking doors. And they’ve got a list.”\r\n\r\nSalee didn’t blink. “What kind.”\r\n\r\n“Names when they can get them. If they can’t, they mark by behavior.” The runner’s eyes flicked to Tiffani’s kit. “They asked for the methodist.”\r\n\r\nBram went still.\r\n\r\nTiffani didn’t.\r\n\r\n“How,” she asked.\r\n\r\n“Clerk said ‘the one who measures cost.’ Like it was praise.” The runner swallowed. “Guard said your name out loud.”\r\n\r\nSo: the Crown didn’t just know she existed.\r\n\r\nIt knew what she did.\r\n\r\nCopper rose behind her tongue—hard, metallic, immediate. The second band in the hum felt heavier than it had a Tide ago, as if the stone had started carrying a new kind of memory.\r\n\r\nHer fingers twitched once around the chalk.\r\n\r\nShe wrote anyway.\r\n\r\n*Observer compromised. Response: accelerate field confirmation. Reduce paper trail.*\r\n\r\nSalee leaned in. “You leave tonight.”\r\n\r\n“I can’t,” Tiffani said. “My access—”\r\n\r\n“Your access is a story you’re telling yourself,” Salee cut in. “They just proved it. You don’t have a key. You have minutes.”\r\n\r\nTiffani shifted her weight before her knee could betray her again. Small correction. Practiced. Not logged.\r\n\r\nBram watched her and didn’t speak. That was worse than teasing. Teasing was denial. Silence was confirmation.\r\n\r\nSalee jerked her chin toward the back room. “Come.”\r\n\r\nThey moved down a narrow hall lined with braces that had been re-set too many times. Each repair was a confession: this place survived by refusing clean failure.\r\n\r\nIn the back room, a man sat at a table with his head down. Both hands were wrapped in cloth.\r\n\r\nHe looked up when they entered.\r\n\r\nHis eyes were too clear—awake in a way that didn’t soften.\r\n\r\nHe didn’t stand. He didn’t greet. He watched the room like it was a problem he already solved once and didn’t want to solve again.\r\n\r\nSalee said one word. “Mobel.”\r\n\r\nBram forgot to breathe for a heartbeat.\r\n\r\nTiffani kept her face neutral and took the room apart: door placement, distances, load-bearing corners, the way the lamp chain wasn’t moving even though the building’s hum had shifted.\r\n\r\nMobel’s gaze went straight to her slate.\r\n\r\n“You’re the one turning people into columns,” he said.\r\n\r\n“I’m the one trying to keep them alive,” Tiffani replied.\r\n\r\nMobel’s mouth moved like it wanted to reject the word and couldn’t find a cleaner substitute. “Alive,” he repeated. “And indexed.”\r\n\r\n“I don’t work for the Crown,” Tiffani said.\r\n\r\nMobel’s eyes slid to Salee and back. “Everyone works for something.”\r\n\r\nTiffani set her slate down open—no concealment, no margin tricks.\r\n\r\n“Jhace Torrins disappeared from an infirmary cot in Saint Merro,” she said. “Ledger page removed. Report burned. Secondary band in the hum. Copper-gold residue concentrating where dispersal says it shouldn’t. He was moved or altered.”\r\n\r\nMobel didn’t flinch.\r\n\r\nThat alone told her she’d hit truth.\r\n\r\n“You know,” Tiffani said.\r\n\r\n“I know enough to tell you your measurement is incomplete,” Mobel replied.\r\n\r\n“I measure manifestation cost,” she said.\r\n\r\n“You measure what shows,” Mobel corrected. “You don’t measure what gets reassigned.”\r\n\r\nTiffani held her chalk still by force. “Reassigned to where.”\r\n\r\nMobel leaned forward. The cloth at his wrists shifted.\r\n\r\nFor one brief window, she saw it—copper-gold under skin, dense and anchored, not threaded the way Jhace’s had been. This wasn’t spread. This was containment.\r\n\r\n“The Brinehammer didn’t only flood streets,” Mobel said. “It changed saturation. Saturation changes trigger thresholds. Holds start earlier. They start without permission.”\r\n\r\n“To where,” Tiffani repeated, refusing to let him stay in principle.\r\n\r\nMobel’s gaze stayed on her hands. “To the inside. To the parts you’re hiding because you think logging makes you weaker.”\r\n\r\nBram exhaled sharp and looked at her fingers like he was watching a gauge needle.\r\n\r\nTiffani didn’t look down.\r\n\r\n“You’re shaking,” Mobel said. Not accusation. Readout.\r\n\r\n“I’m functional,” Tiffani said.\r\n\r\nMobel’s eyes shifted to Salee. “They came for me before. They’ll come again. Faster now. The city needs someone to blame for stone failing.”\r\n\r\nSalee’s voice dropped. “They’re already asking for her.”\r\n\r\nMobel nodded once. “Then you either disappear into the Dregs and become rumor, or you go find Jhace and become evidence.”\r\n\r\nTiffani’s chest tightened. “Where.”\r\n\r\nMobel’s gaze flicked to the knotted textile on the table. “Your vector is right. But you’re chasing the wrong answer.”\r\n\r\nTiffani held still. “Explain.”\r\n\r\n“You think I’m the solution,” Mobel said. “I’m not. I’m the warning. Jhace is the anomaly now. Someone did a procedure on him and moved him like equipment.”\r\n\r\nBram’s voice cracked low. “Who.”\r\n\r\nMobel looked past them, toward the wall, as if listening through the stone. The hum thickened. The lamp chain finally gave one short sway, then stopped.\r\n\r\n“Not Crown,” Mobel said. “Crown is loud. This is quiet.”\r\n\r\nHe didn’t offer a name. That wasn’t mercy. That was survival practice.\r\n\r\nTiffani wrote:\r\n\r\n*Non-Crown actor suspected. Capability: procedure + suppression + wake correction.*\r\n\r\nMobel’s eyes snapped to the slate. “Stop writing that.”\r\n\r\nTiffani’s chalk froze mid-stroke.\r\n\r\nSalee said, flat, “She writes to stay steady.”\r\n\r\nMobel said, “She writes to make herself traceable.”\r\n\r\nSilence held long enough to become a weight.\r\n\r\nThen boots stopped outside.\r\n\r\nNot refugee shuffling. Not the Dregs’ quick routing. This was coordinated. Spaced. Confident.\r\n\r\nSalee moved without a word. Hand signal. Two runners vanished. Someone killed a lamp. The room didn’t go dark; it went intentional.\r\n\r\nBram’s voice dropped to almost nothing. “That’s them.”\r\n\r\nTiffani didn’t look at the door. She listened: belt metal, baton scrape, a breath held too long by someone trained to wait.\r\n\r\nThis wasn’t harvesting.\r\n\r\nThis was collection.\r\n\r\nMobel stood.\r\n\r\nThe cloth around his hands tightened as his fingers flexed under it—restraint, not tremor.\r\n\r\nHe looked at Tiffani. “Now you log your cost.”\r\n\r\n“I don’t have time,” she said.\r\n\r\n“You never do,” Mobel replied. “That’s how they win.”\r\n\r\nThe latch screamed as pressure hit it in sequence—controlled, practiced.\r\n\r\nSalee’s voice went razor-low. “Back corridor. Now.”\r\n\r\nTiffani grabbed her slate. Bram grabbed her kit. Salee pulled a loose board from the wall like it had been waiting for this moment.\r\n\r\nThe second band in the hum surged hard enough to narrow Tiffani’s vision for a heartbeat.\r\n\r\nHer knee locked.\r\n\r\nThis time it didn’t ask permission.\r\n\r\nShe caught herself on the table edge. The chalk snapped in her grip. Pain flashed up her wrist.\r\n\r\nMobel saw it.\r\n\r\n“Good,” he said, and it came out ugly. “Now it’s real to you.”\r\n\r\nThe board came free. Cold air spilled in—damp, brine-loaded, carrying wet-rope stink and the mineral bite of flooded stone.\r\n\r\nBram went through first.\r\n\r\nTiffani followed.\r\n\r\nBehind them, the door gave.\r\n\r\nNot a crash. A release. Like a restraint cut.\r\n\r\nAs Tiffani crawled into the back corridor, water dripped somewhere above—steady, patient, not enough to drown you yet. The Brinehammer wasn’t in this room, but it had seeped into everything that mattered: movement, authority, thresholds, and the price of being seen.\r\n\r\nAnd behind her, the Crown stepped into the room where truth had been sitting at a table.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\"\"\"\r\n\r\nout_path = Path(\"/mnt/data/N02_Light_Cracks_Through_Ch1-12.md\")\r\nout_path.write_text(content, encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\nprint(out_path)"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1767958101.993258,
        "end_turn": false,
        "id": "4342add3-9090-418f-aeee-d9db77bc8a95",

    "514e65f8-0f70-4d95-b533-dc88abbea332": {
      "children": [

      "id": "514e65f8-0f70-4d95-b533-dc88abbea332",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "chapter_texts={}\nfor i in range(1,13):\n    seg = chapters[i]\n    # get title line up to newline\n    title_line = seg.splitlines()[0]\n    full = \"## CHAPTER \" + seg\n    chapter_texts[i]=full\n    wc = len(re.findall(r\"\\b[\\w’'-]+\\b\", full))\n    print(i, title_line.strip(), wc)\n"
        },

    "5181c72b-6d58-4772-a8ea-0b59d2dba571": {
      "children": [

      ],
      "id": "5181c72b-6d58-4772-a8ea-0b59d2dba571",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "chapter_counts=[]\nfor num, body in chapters:\n    w = re.findall(r\"\\b[\\w’'-]+\\b\", body)\n    chapter_counts.append((int(num), len(w)))\nchapter_counts[:5], sum(c for _,c in chapter_counts)\n"
        },

    "54487dcf-bb1c-4e15-a462-07933b895692": {
      "children": [
        "1e4ec79b-adcd-42bd-aade-4f94e550e515"
      ],
      "id": "54487dcf-bb1c-4e15-a462-07933b895692",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "## CHAPTER 7: TRACKING\n\n*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T6*\n\nThe coast smelled wrong—brine, rot, and silt that belonged under stone, not on top of it.\n\nJhace walked north with his kit tight against his ribs. Phase lag made every step arrive late. He corrected by counting and refusing to rush.\n\nMobel’s wake stayed readable: dock pilings braced too clean, warehouse walls stabilized mid-fail with residue concentrated at joints, stone stairs corrected without the scuff patterns tremoring hands left behind.\n\nThe Brinehammer made stress points everywhere.\n\nThat made the shard behind his sternum restless.\n\nHe refused three holds before noon and paid for refusal anyway. Heat built. Phase lag worsened. His hands stayed steady while the rest of him felt less aligned.\n\nAt a coastal settlement, a pier crane leaned toward collapse. A soaked man stood under it and stared up without moving.\n\nJhace walked past.\n\nTen steps later the shard surged without permission.\n\nLight pressed out through his ribs into stone. The crane joint locked. The lean stopped.\n\nJhace cut coupling immediately and kept moving.\n\nHeat punched his chest. Copper flooded his mouth. When he wiped his wrist, a thin smear of blood came away. Not a wound—internal spill.\n\nHis palm glowed brighter. Copper-gold threads climbed another finger’s width toward his wrist.\n\nHe wrote while walking:\n\n*A5-T6. Involuntary hold triggered by proximity + saturation stress. Cost: heat spike + oral bleed + spread accelerated.*\n\nWitnesses spoke about “the calm one” who fixed a brace and didn’t shake afterward. Mobel. Two or three Tides ahead, depending on who remembered truth and who remembered what kept them safe.\n\nRefugees moved inland along the road—carts rattling, bundles wet, children carried too high to keep them out of water. One woman saw the glow under Jhace’s sleeve and backed away like he was an exposed edge.\n\nJhace pulled his sleeve down and kept moving.\n\nMobel’s wake led toward higher ground—fresh residue at a culvert, clean joint locks on a bridge support undermined by floodwater.\n\nJhace followed.\n\nThe pressure behind his sternum didn’t ease.\n\nIf anything, it felt more certain.\n\n---\n\n## CHAPTER 8: ENTROPY\n\n*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T7*\n\nVelocity-Heights sat above the Dregs on newer stone and older arrogance.\n\nIt didn’t take the Brinehammer as water in streets. It took it as bodies at gates.\n\nRefugees were being funneled into glass-roofed halls and “temporary” shelters built from lattice and pane. The architecture looked precise. The loads were not. Wet bundles, too many feet, panic weight shifting without rhythm.\n\nCorlexi arrived alone under cover: merchant verification, relief routing assessment.\n\nReal purpose: Eries.\n\nHe found her in a public garden where glass lattice cut sunlight into hard lines across stone. Students sat around her with open slates.\n\n“The Brinehammer isn’t a moral event,” Eries said. “It isn’t punishment. It’s force meeting structure. If your structure lies to itself, force corrects the lie.”\n\nWhen the students dispersed, Eries didn’t turn to Corlexi immediately.\n\n“You’re not here for shipping,” she said.\n\n“Crown,” she added, and didn’t wait for an answer.\n\n“You write about decay,” Corlexi said.\n\n“I write about reality,” Eries replied. “People want blame because blame feels like control.”\n\n“The registry prevents uncontrolled risk.”\n\n“The registry labels risk,” Eries said. “After it’s already moving.”\n\nA shout rose beyond the garden wall. A relief crew dragged glass panes toward a shelter hall. The panes were strapped wrong. The load shifted. The top sheet flexed and began to bow.\n\nA crack snapped through the air and the sheet started to split.\n\nPeople scattered. Some didn’t move fast enough.\n\nEries stepped into the churn and said one word—“Down”—and the nearest students dropped without argument.\n\nCorlexi read the stress line instantly. The glass wasn’t annealed for this transport. It was built to sit, not be hauled through panic.\n\nHe could correct it with Form.\n\nHe also knew Crown guards were watching.\n\nHe moved anyway.\n\nHands on a wooden brace like any other man steadying a load. Under that physical cover, he adjusted strap path and brace angle with Form—small correction, clean redistribution.\n\nThe glass stopped singing. The split halted as a hairline.\n\nA guard stared directly at him.\n\nEries watched too, not surprised—confirming.\n\nWhen the crisis passed, Eries returned to the path and wiped glass dust from her palm.\n\n“You intervened,” she said.\n\n“I prevented waste.”\n\n“You prevented blood,” Eries corrected. “Without visible cost markers.”\n\nCorlexi kept his voice flat. “Observation is a habit.”\n\n“Reality is,” Eries said. “Your registry won’t stop holds from failing. It will decide who gets blamed when they do.”\n\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1767959335.5815876,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "54487dcf-bb1c-4e15-a462-07933b895692",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "final_expression_output": "## CHAPTER 7: TRACKING\n\n*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T6*\n\nThe coast smelled wrong—brine, rot, and silt that belonged under stone, not on top of it.\n\nJhace walked north with his kit tight against his ribs. Phase lag made every step arrive late. He corrected by counting and refusing to rush.\n\nMobel’s wake stayed readable: dock pilings braced too clean, warehouse walls stabilized mid-fail with residue concentrated at joints, stone stairs corrected without the scuff patterns tremoring hands left behind.\n\nThe Brinehammer made stress points everywhere.\n\nThat made the shard behind his sternum restless.\n\nHe refused three holds before noon and paid for refusal anyway. Heat built. Phase lag worsened. His hands stayed steady while the rest of him felt less aligned.\n\nAt a coastal settlement, a pier crane leaned toward collapse. A soaked man stood under it and stared up without moving.\n\nJhace walked past.\n\nTen steps later the shard surged without permission.\n\nLight pressed out through his ribs into stone. The crane joint locked. The lean stopped.\n\nJhace cut coupling immediately and kept moving.\n\nHeat punched his chest. Copper flooded his mouth. When he wiped his wrist, a thin smear of blood came away. Not a wound—internal spill.\n\nHis palm glowed brighter. Copper-gold threads climbed another finger’s width toward his wrist.\n\nHe wrote while walking:\n\n*A5-T6. Involuntary hold triggered by proximity + saturation stress. Cost: heat spike + oral bleed + spread accelerated.*\n\nWitnesses spoke about “the calm one” who fixed a brace and didn’t shake afterward. Mobel. Two or three Tides ahead, depending on who remembered truth and who remembered what kept them safe.\n\nRefugees moved inland along the road—carts rattling, bundles wet, children carried too high to keep them out of water. One woman saw the glow under Jhace’s sleeve and backed away like he was an exposed edge.\n\nJhace pulled his sleeve down and kept moving.\n\nMobel’s wake led toward higher ground—fresh residue at a culvert, clean joint locks on a bridge support undermined by floodwater.\n\nJhace followed.\n\nThe pressure behind his sternum didn’t ease.\n\nIf anything, it felt more certain.\n\n---\n\n## CHAPTER 8: ENTROPY\n\n*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T7*\n\nVelocity-Heights sat above the Dregs on newer stone and older arrogance.\n\nIt didn’t take the Brinehammer as water in streets. It took it as bodies at gates.\n\nRefugees were being funneled into glass-roofed halls and “temporary” shelters built from lattice and pane. The architecture looked precise. The loads were not. Wet bundles, too many feet, panic weight shifting without rhythm.\n\nCorlexi arrived alone under cover: merchant verification, relief routing assessment.\n\nReal purpose: Eries.\n\nHe found her in a public garden where glass lattice cut sunlight into hard lines across stone. Students sat around her with open slates.\n\n“The Brinehammer isn’t a moral event,” Eries said. “It isn’t punishment. It’s force meeting structure. If your structure lies to itself, force corrects the lie.”\n\nWhen the students dispersed, Eries didn’t turn to Corlexi immediately.\n\n“You’re not here for shipping,” she said.\n\n“Crown,” she added, and didn’t wait for an answer.\n\n“You write about decay,” Corlexi said.\n\n“I write about reality,” Eries replied. “People want blame because blame feels like control.”\n\n“The registry prevents uncontrolled risk.”\n\n“The registry labels risk,” Eries said. “After it’s already moving.”\n\nA shout rose beyond the garden wall. A relief crew dragged glass panes toward a shelter hall. The panes were strapped wrong. The load shifted. The top sheet flexed and began to bow.\n\nA crack snapped through the air and the sheet started to split.\n\nPeople scattered. Some didn’t move fast enough.\n\nEries stepped into the churn and said one word—“Down”—and the nearest students dropped without argument.\n\nCorlexi read the stress line instantly. The glass wasn’t annealed for this transport. It was built to sit, not be hauled through panic.\n\nHe could correct it with Form.\n\nHe also knew Crown guards were watching.\n\nHe moved anyway.\n\nHands on a wooden brace like any other man steadying a load. Under that physical cover, he adjusted strap path and brace angle with Form—small correction, clean redistribution.\n\nThe glass stopped singing. The split halted as a hairline.\n\nA guard stared directly at him.\n\nEries watched too, not surprised—confirming.\n\nWhen the crisis passed, Eries returned to the path and wiped glass dust from her palm.\n\n“You intervened,” she said.\n\n“I prevented waste.”\n\n“You prevented blood,” Eries corrected. “Without visible cost markers.”\n\nCorlexi kept his voice flat. “Observation is a habit.”\n\n“Reality is,” Eries said. “Your registry won’t stop holds from failing. It will decide who gets blamed when they do.”\n\n",

    "5474844e-62e1-4aec-b462-efb8c1734c0c": {
      "children": [
        "7b388fde-e652-41bc-871e-9a3d62e295f8"

      "id": "5474844e-62e1-4aec-b462-efb8c1734c0c",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "## CHAPTER 4: SEARCHING\n\n*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T3*\n\nHe woke expecting paralysis.\n\nHis fingers moved.\n\nJhace stayed still and ran the test the way he ran every inspection: smallest system first, then expand.\n\nRight hand: functional. Delay of three heartbeats between intention and motion.  \nLeft hand: functional. Same delay.  \nToes: responsive, but late—as if signals had been rerouted through bad junctions.  \nLegs: sensation present. Not memory. Present.\n\nThe tremor was gone.\n\nHe raised his hands into thin light. Steady. No flutter at the wrist. No shake in the knuckles.\n\nRelief didn’t arrive.\n\nHeat sat behind his sternum instead. Constant pressure, not pain. Too tight to ignore, too steady to call “temporary.”\n\nHe sat up.\n\nThe room tilted, corrected a heartbeat late. His body lagged behind command. He didn’t fight it. Fighting made mistakes.\n\nNot the infirmary.\n\nA narrow maintenance room. Cold pipe run on one wall. Low steel table bolted to the floor. Dust in corners. Chalk scuffs on stone.\n\nHis secondary slate lay on the table. Calibration strap beside it. Three copper-stained chalk pieces set in a line, tips aligned.\n\nNot dumped. Placed.\n\nHis primary slate was missing.\n\nHe remembered leaving it behind—the heavy one, the one that could become evidence in the wrong hands. He’d left it where “admission” could be altered and blamed.\n\nHe looked at his palm.\n\nCopper-gold light sat under the skin in thin threads that weren’t veins and weren’t ink. Flex: brighter. Still: dimmer. Never gone.\n\nMemory came in strips.\n\nThe cot. No voice. No movement. Breathing and waiting.\n\nA figure in the doorway.\n\nNot shadowed. Not lit. Light behaved wrong around it.\n\nA burn in his palm.\n\nThe sensation of being mapped—nerve, vessel, joint—surveyed and set.\n\nNo face. No voice. No reason.\n\nOnly certainty: it wasn’t rescue.\n\nIt was procedure.\n\nA low vibration rolled through the structure. Heavy enough to make the pipe run above him tremble.\n\nThen the impact—deep, muffled, clean through stone.\n\nPressure followed. The steel table complained against its bolts. Dust fell from a ceiling seam.\n\nHe stood too fast. Lag punished him; his legs accepted the command late and he caught himself on the pipe.\n\nHeat surged behind his sternum. Copper flooded his mouth.\n\nBrine rode in it.\n\nHe strapped his kit on by muscle memory. Buckles clicked. Strap bit his shoulder. Good. Real.\n\nHe wrote one line on the secondary slate:\n\n*A5-T3. Mobility restored. Tremor absent. Phase lag present. Core pressure constant.*\n\nHe tested coupling.\n\nFocused on a pipe bracket.\n\nThe coupling didn’t start in his hands.\n\nIt started behind his sternum. Tight bloom, outward push. Light bled through ribs into metal and wall. He felt stress in the joint without touching it.\n\nHe pushed.\n\nThe bracket settled. Pipe vibration smoothed. Structure complied.\n\nHeat hit hard enough to soften his vision at the edges.\n\nHe broke coupling immediately.\n\nCost: heat. Visual edge loss. Phase lag worsening post-hold.\n\nHe wrote:\n\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1767959465.5746691,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "5474844e-62e1-4aec-b462-efb8c1734c0c",
