        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "```python\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\ntext = r\"\"\"# N02: LIGHT CRACKS THROUGH  \n**Turning:** GLASS (G)  \n**Timeline:** A5-T2 through A5-T11 (primary on-page window)  \n**POV Roster:** Tiffani Merrow (Track 1) • Corlexi (Track 2) • Jhace Torrins (Track 3)  \n**Structure:** 12 chapters  \n\n---\n\n# CHAPTER 1: MISSING  \n*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T2*\n\nThe cot was empty.\n\nTiffani stopped in the doorway with her slate braced against her forearm and the chalk already poised. She had a three-Tide plan for him. Breathing rate. Tremor amplitude. Pupil response. Autonomic drift. Everything that would establish a usable baseline for Stage Four progression before the body started cutting corners she couldn’t document back into order.\n\nThe sheets weren’t shoved aside.\n\nThey were folded. Corner to corner. Tight. Clean.\n\nA paralyzed man did not fold sheets.\n\nShe stepped in and let her eyes do what they did best: turn a room into counts. Cold stone underfoot. No grit under her boots. No scuff line where a heel dragged.\n\nCup on the side table—placed at arm’s reach from where his shoulder would have been. He couldn’t have reached it yesterday. The calibration strap was gone. So was his secondary slate. Three pieces of copper-stained chalk, the ones he kept separate because the residue read cleaner when you weren’t cross-contaminating with common white.\n\nHis primary measurement slate remained.\n\nThe heavy one.\n\nThe one that mattered.\n\nShe wrote, neat and uniform:\n\n*Cot empty. Sheets folded. Cup repositioned. Tools taken: calibration strap, secondary slate, chalk (3 pcs, copper-stained). Tools left: primary slate.*\n\nHer hand stayed steady. She kept her eyes on the slate while she wrote. Looking at absence invited speculation. Speculation wasn’t data.\n\nFootsteps halted in the corridor. Not careful. Not loud. The latch clicked.\n\nA medical clerk edged into the doorway—young, trying to look like his spine had been issued with the uniform.\n\n“The director wants to see you. About records.”\n\n“After.” Tiffani didn’t turn. “Explain the patient evacuation process.”\n\n“There wasn’t one.” His mouth tightened at one corner. “No evacuation authorization filed. No transfer order. No discharge.”\n\nTiffani’s chalk paused.\n\n“Then he’s still documented as admitted.”\n\n“That’s the problem.” The clerk swallowed. Once, then again. “Someone removed the admission ledger page. The director is… unclear what protocol is, now.”\n\nTiffani wrote, smaller, where it wouldn’t be the first thing read if her slate was seized:\n\n*Institutional obstruction: logged.*\n\nThe clerk left fast, as if speed could become innocence.\n\nShe waited six heartbeats—enough time for panic to get down the hall and start infecting other people—then went to the guard.\n\nHe was posted outside the door. Young. Fresh uniform. New fabric still holding its fold.\n\n“Patient Jhace Torrins,” she said. “Stage Four. Admitted A4-T19. Paralysis from sternum down. Motor function nonexistent. When did he leave?”\n\nThe guard snapped straighter. “He didn’t. No one entered. No one left. I’ve been here since second bell.”\n\n“The door was watched the entire time.”\n\n“Yes, ma’am.”\n\n“No breaks. No distractions. No message that required you to look away.”\n\nHis eyes flicked once—down the corridor, then back. His fingers tightened on the door frame. “I checked the corridor. Standard sweep. Maybe forty heartbeats.”\n\n“Forty,” Tiffani said, and wrote it down.\n\nWhile her chalk moved, she watched him the way she watched structures under load. Breath shallow. Pupils wide for a corridor that bright. Knuckles pale.\n\nHe was either lying.\n\nOr he’d seen something he didn’t have language for and had decided silence was safer than truth.\n\nShe didn’t press. Pressure changed behavior. Changed behavior ruined reads.\n\n“Thank you,” she said, and left him with his fear.\n\nBack inside, she shut the door and stood in the space where the impossible had happened.\n\nThe grid reference-hum in this wing carried a second mode. It sat under the normal vibration in the stone—lower, deeper, persistent. She’d noticed it yesterday. She hadn’t written it because noticing wasn’t the same as confirming.\n\nCopper rose behind her tongue. Stronger than yesterday.\n\nHer fingers twitched once as she lifted the chalk.\n\nShe did not add that line to the slate.\n\nIf he moved, there was a vector. If someone moved him, there was evidence. Her job was to make the room confess without asking it politely.\n\nShe started at the threshold and worked clockwise.\n\nDoorframe: no fresh marks. Wall: no shoulder smear, no cloth rub, no grit ground into plaster at carry height. Floor: no drag line. Window: latched, dust on the sill undisturbed.\n\nThe ash bin by the cold stove held half-burned paper. She knelt and sifted with two fingers. Most of it collapsed into black powder. One piece held long enough to read:\n\n*…locked maintenance corridor…requires two hands + walking…impossible…*\n\nA report.\n\nSomeone had written it.\n\nSomeone had decided it needed to vanish.\n\nShe slid the scrap into her notes.\n\nFootsteps again. Faster this time. Not careful.\n\nBram appeared in the doorway, coat rumpled. His face tried to assemble a joke and quit halfway.\n\n“Cool,” he said, staring at the empty cot. “Love walking into rooms where the paralyzed guy quits his job and leaves.”\n\n“Not left.” Tiffani stood. Ash dust clung to her fingertips; she wiped it onto her trousers without looking. “Moved. Method unknown.”\n\nBram’s jaw worked. “That is not better.”\n\n“It’s accurate.”\n\nHe dropped onto a crate beside the cot. The wood complained. “Tiff. Yesterday he couldn’t move his fingers. Today he’s gone. That’s not clerical.”\n\n“No. It’s authority.” She kept her voice level because anger was loud and the building liked to listen. “Someone with reach removed a ledger page and burned a report.”\n\nShe lifted Jhace’s primary slate—tested the weight, the worn edge where his thumb always rode. No note. No instruction. Just deliberate placement.\n\nA control variable left behind.\n\nShe set it back exactly where it had been.\n\nBram watched her do it. “So what now?”\n\n“Now I follow physics,” Tiffani said. “Residue. Mode surges. Stress anomalies. He didn’t evaporate. He left a trail or someone else did.”\n\n“And if he doesn’t want to be found?”\n\nShe didn’t answer. Want was not a measurable property. Her method didn’t have a column for will.\n\nShe packed instead. Slate. Chalk. Scrap. Calipers. The small wedge gauge she used when she needed the world to admit angles.\n\nBram stood. “You’re going to get us into something bad.”\n\n“Define bad.”\n\n“The kind where we disappear and somebody burns our paperwork too.”\n\nTiffani closed the kit. The buckles snapped, clean and final. “Then we document faster than they can destroy.”\n\nThey left the room together. The guard remained at his post. She didn’t look at him.\n\nSeven incidents in three Tides. Seven holds that should have failed. Copper-gold residue where it didn’t belong. A second mode in the hum that followed a pattern.\n\nSomeone was working without paying the visible cost markers.\n\nSomeone was leaving proof.\n\nTiffani started down the stairs. Her hand trembled once against the rail.\n\nHer right knee caught on the third step, locked, then released.\n\nShe kept walking.\n\nShe left both facts off the slate.\n\n---\n\nThe building took a hit.\n\nNot a crack of impact—no sharp report, no ringing. A low, muffled bass punch that arrived through the soles of her boots and climbed her shins into her teeth. The stairwell flexed. Dust sifted from a seam overhead. Lamps swung on their chains, lazy at first, then wider.\n\nBram froze mid-step, one hand lifted like it could steady the air.\n\nTiffani stopped breathing long enough to listen with her whole body.\n\nThe grid reference-hum stuttered. The second mode swelled hard, then dipped, then settled into something heavier.\n\n“Quiet,” Tiffani said, and meant it.\n\nThey stood while the city decided whether it was done being violent.\n\nFor four heartbeats the only sound was lamp-chain creak and distant shouting that hadn’t learned the new emergency yet.\n\nThen came pressure. Not impact. Weight. A broad shove that made the stairwell door tremble in its frame.\n\nThe hinges squealed.\n\nThe seam under the door darkened.\n\nWater forced itself through first as a line, then as fingers, then as a palm. Cold. Salt-sharp. Laced with silt. It smelled of sea rot and crushed algae and harbor sludge scraped up from below.\n\nBram backed up. “No. No, no—”\n\nThe door bucked. The frame split at a crack that had been there before today and had just been given a reason to open. Water punched through in a sheet and hit the stairwell wall hard enough to throw spray across the stone.\n\nGlass shattered somewhere above them—one of the narrow corridor windows giving up. The next surge came through that opening and down the stairs in a grinding rush that carried splinters, rope ends, and a plank spinning end-over-end.\n\nPeople screamed on the landing below. The sound cut short as the water found them.\n\nTiffani grabbed Bram by the collar and yanked him up, away from the stairwell mouth, toward higher steps. Her kit slammed her hip with each movement. Cold brine licked at the treads behind them.\n\nSaint Merro had been built to keep the sea outside.\n\nThe sea had revised the plan.\n\nThe Brinehammer was in the streets now, and the city’s stonework was about to learn what force looked like when it didn’t care about authority, ledgers, or locks.\n\n---\n\n# CHAPTER 2: FORM  \n*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T2*\n\nThe room smelled of chalk dust and old stone.\n\nCorlexi sat at a narrow table that served two lives. One life was grain tallies, transport costs, and the harmless boredom of a merchant. The other was pinned to the walls in colored ink and metal points: incident maps, cost progression curves, termination thresholds, name lists compiled without consent because consent was a luxury the Crown didn’t bother buying.\n\nA knock came—wrong timing, wrong rhythm.\n\nHe moved without haste. Charts slid under a merchant ledger. His shoulders settled into the posture that invited no second look.\n\nAn older woman stood in the doorway. Textile calluses. Eyes that didn’t waste motion.\n\n“Message from Salee,” she said. “She says you’re documenting the Dregs.”\n\n“I document inventory,” Corlexi replied. “Documentation keeps business from bleeding.”\n\n“Salee says documentation is business.” The woman’s gaze stayed fixed on his face. “She wanted you to know she sees you.”\n\nShe left before he could waste a response.\n\nCorlexi waited until the footfall faded, then unbuttoned his shirt to the waist.\n\nThe scars cut diagonal across his torso—pale seams where copper-gold had fused into tissue during the Piercing. Twelve fragments, each no larger than a thumbnail. He had counted them enough times that the number lived behind his eyes.\n\nHe took a breath and tested.\n\nNo tremor.\n\nNo exhaustion drift.\n\nNo vein-lines crawling up his forearms.\n\nHe focused on the crooked lamp bracket across the room. He read it. Iron. Stress at the joint where the mount met the wall. Load tolerance still adequate, but the fasteners had been giving way by degrees for at least two Anneals.\n\nHe adjusted the structure.\n\nThe bracket straightened with a soft click and settled as if it had never been wrong.\n\nForm. Structure maintained.\n\nNo visible filament light. No copper spill. No cost marker. The room stayed quiet except for chain sway.\n\nThat absence made him valuable.\n\nIt also made him a problem that could be solved.\n\nHe buttoned up and pulled the collapse reports back into the open.\n\nSeven incidents in three Tides. Saint Merro took most of them—still bleeding from the Piercing, still trying to pretend the grid reference-hum was “normal” again. But the holds were wrong. Too clean. Too complete. Casualty projections that should have been met—and weren’t. Residue concentrated in ways standard dispersal didn’t produce.\n\nSomeone else was operating outside the visible cost curve.\n\nHe wrote on his slate, not as speculation but as a working risk:\n\n*Pattern suggests internal shard integration may suppress standard cost markers. Requires confirmation. Additional subjects needed.*\n\nA faint vibration rolled through the wall. Not ambient drift. Something low and distant, like stone being informed that it might have to move. He didn’t look at the window. Looking was a tell.\n\nThe hallway outside filled with hurried feet. Not panicked—directed. Directed movement meant a network, and networks meant Salee.\n\nA second knock, harder.\n\nCorlexi swept the charts under the ledger again and opened the door to find a runner with salt on his cuffs and a textile knot tied to his wrist.\n\n“Saint Merro got hit,” the runner said. “Water in the streets. People moving. Refugees coming.”\n\nCorlexi let his face stay dull, merchant-appropriate. “Why tell me.”\n\n“Because you write things down,” the runner said. “And because writing means planning, and planning means we don’t die stupid.”\n\nThe runner shoved a folded list into Corlexi’s hand. Not names. Needs. Rope. Boards. Dry cloth. Nails. Bread. Something to brace doors that had stopped being doors.\n\nCorlexi looked at the list and felt, for the first time, the registry become useful in the way knives were useful. Not moral. Practical.\n\nHe put the list under the grain ledger, then asked, “Where’s Salee.”\n\n“Routing the first wave,” the runner said. “Also—your building is taking load.”\n\n“Load.”\n\n“People,” the runner snapped. “Too many. Too fast. Floors aren’t meant for it.”\n\nThe runner left. Corlexi stood in the doorway and listened to his own building.\n\nStone carried the mass clean. Wood carried it late. The disagreement made small noises he’d ignored for months. Now it mattered.\n\nHe moved down the stairs and into the lower room where residents were already packing. Not a formal shelter—just a place people knew would still have a roof when the Dregs’ patched structures gave out.\n\nA woman with brine-stiff hair shoved a bundle under a table. A man hauled a child up onto a bench. Someone slammed the door and the frame answered with a complaint.\n\nCorlexi saw it: the central beam had a hairline split at the join, old damage made worse by the sudden shift in weight. It would hold another hour. Maybe two. Then it would let go and take a corner of the floor with it.\n\nIf it dropped, people would panic. Panic turned into uncontrolled coupling. Uncontrolled coupling turned into bodies.\n\nHe could intervene.\n\nHe could also be seen.\n\nSalee’s people were in this room. He recognized the quiet ones: eyes that tracked exits, hands that touched beams without looking like they were checking them.\n\nCorlexi moved like a merchant checking inventory. He set down a crate, leaned, and let his focus slide into the split join.\n\nForm. Quiet. Surgical.\n\nHe didn’t “fix” it. He redistributed load just enough to buy time. The split stopped widening. The beam’s vibration steadied.\n\nCost: none visible.\n\nRisk: immediate.\n\nA man near the wall turned his head sharply, as if he’d heard something that wasn’t a sound. The runner with the textile knot looked at Corlexi’s hands.\n\nCorlexi straightened, picked up a sack of grain, and made a show of irritation. “If you’re stacking bodies in here, stack them against the stone wall, not mid-span. You want the floor to keep being a floor.”\n\nThe instruction sounded like merchant complaint.\n\nIt was also correct.\n\nPeople shifted where they stood. The load redistributed again, now by human choice. That mattered. It meant his adjustment would be blamed on common sense, not on anomaly.\n\nThe runner nodded once, almost imperceptible, and moved on.\n\nCorlexi returned upstairs and wrote a single line on a merchant sheet:\n\n*Saint Merro inundation event initiated. Dregs taking refugee load. Structural failure risk increases. Cover risk increases with emergency intervention.*\n\nThen, smaller, where it could hide:\n\n*Salee sees me. Now her people may have felt me.*\n\nHe folded the page under grain totals and resumed the posture of a man who cared only about prices.\n\n---\n\n# CHAPTER 3: WAKE  \n*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T3 through A5-T7*\n\nSeven reports. Seven incidents. Three Tides.\n\nTiffani laid them across her workspace and didn’t let herself blink too long. Patterns didn’t reveal themselves to people who looked away.\n\nSeam collapse near Junction Twelve boundary. Estimated hold duration: two hundred heartbeats. Zero casualties where her structural projection demanded four minimum. Copper-gold residue concentrated at load junctions instead of fanning out through surrounding material.\n\nFoundation stabilization at Merchant Row—same residue behavior.\n\nBridge support failure at the southern canal—again.\n\nEach site carried a mode surge in the grid reference-hum that didn’t match ambient drift. Not random noise. A signature.\n\nShe plotted locations on a city map and drew lines with a tool made for straight truth. The geometry didn’t argue.\n\nThe vector pointed northeast.\n\nGrindheim.\n\nA quiet scrape at her door. Not a knock. Someone trying not to be recorded by sound.\n\nCorran stepped in and shut it behind him. Mid-level Guild administration. Tired eyes. Restricted stamp on the folder in his hand. The expression of a man walking into a decision he already hated.\n\n“Unofficially,” he said, “you did not get this from me.”\n\nHe set the folder down and didn’t sit.\n\n“The director is calling it clerical loss,” Corran continued. “Lost ledger pages. Misfiled incident routing. Your search authorization gets revoked next Tide.”\n\nTiffani stood and went to the archive cabinet on instinct. Pulled the Saint Merro incident drawer.\n\nIt didn’t open.\n\nNot stuck—locked. Fresh metal. New keyway.\n\nCorran didn’t look at the lock. He stared at the floor like the floor might offer forgiveness.\n\n“They moved fast,” Tiffani said.\n\n“They moved before I could stop them,” Corran replied.\n\nTiffani let her fingers rest on the new lock just long enough to feel the cold. Then she stepped back.\n\n“Good,” she said. “Now it’s provable.”\n\nCorran’s throat worked. “Someone doesn’t want you following this.”\n\nTiffani opened the folder.\n\nEighth incident. Grindheim Dregs. Old textile district. Same residue concentration. Same hold cleanliness. Same missing casualties.\n\nHer chalk hovered over her slate.\n\n“Why help me?” she asked.\n\nCorran’s voice dropped. “Because your ledger page wasn’t ‘lost.’ It was removed. I saw the request in the archive before it got scrubbed. Administrative channel. Crown-level reach.”\n\nInstitutional obstruction: confirmed.\n\nInstitutional interference: resourced.\n\nTiffani kept her face still. “What do you want.”\n\n“When you understand what happened—when you find out what was done to him—send word outside the official channels.” Corran didn’t offer a method. He didn’t need to. “Don’t send it where they can erase it.”\n\nHe left without waiting for agreement. That, too, was a data point.\n\nTwo Tides later she found Bram in the Guild hall with a chipped cup and something dark inside it.\n\n“I need access to Grindheim,” she said. “Before the revocation lands. You have textile ties and harbor adjacency. Get me a reason that opens doors.”\n\nBram looked up like his body wanted to make a joke and his brain filed a denial. “I’m not a spy. I’m barely functional.”\n\n“You can move where I can’t,” she said. “And people talk when you make them laugh.”\n\nHe exhaled. “That is… accurate.”\n\n“Get me in.”\n\nHis cup hit the table once, hard enough to rattle the saucer. “When this goes bad, I’m haunting you. I’m going to move your chalk. I’m going to rearrange your kit. I’m going to make your straight lines crooked.”\n\nTiffani didn’t look up from her slate. “Accepted.”\n\nShe returned to her workspace and finished the map.\n\nEight incidents. Then nine, after another report slid to her through a quiet chain of hands that avoided stamps and signatures. The vector held. Grindheim’s Dregs. A place where buildings leaned because they had to, and information moved through people instead of paperwork.\n\nAs she drew the final line, copper surged behind her tongue.\n\nHer right knee locked mid-motion, released, then refused to cooperate for six heartbeats.\n\nShe gripped the desk edge until function returned.\n\nShe did not write either fact down.\n\nIf she logged it, she lost the work. If she lost the work, he stayed missing. So she didn’t log it.\n\nShe packed her kit. Slate. Chalk. The burned scrap. The incident reports that existed outside official circulation because someone decided truth didn’t qualify as permitted knowledge.\n\nTomorrow they left for Grindheim.\n\nTomorrow she followed the physics trail into a district where institutions held no power and networks held all of it.\n\nTomorrow she became unauthorized on purpose.\n\nAt the bottom of the stairs outside her workspace, she paused and wrote one line in a personal margin she never showed anyone:\n\n*Jhace chose departure. Authority chose concealment. Physics will decide who’s lying.*\n\nHer hand shook while she wrote it.\n\nShe still left the tremor off the slate.\n\n---\n\n# CHAPTER 4: SEARCHING  \n*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T3*\n\nHe woke expecting paralysis.\n\nHis fingers moved.\n\nJhace lay still and ran the test in the only order that ever kept him alive: smallest system first, then expand.\n\nRight hand: functional. Delay of three heartbeats between intention and motion.  \nLeft hand: functional. Same delay.  \nToes: responsive, but the signal arrived late, as if the line had been rerouted through bad junctions.  \nLegs: sensation present. Not remembered—present.\n\nThe tremor was gone.\n\nHe raised both hands into the thin light and watched them. Steady. No shake in the knuckles. No flutter at the wrist.\n\nThat should have felt like relief.\n\nIt didn’t.\n\nSomething else sat in its place—heat behind his sternum, constant and wrong. Not fever. Not pain. Pressure, like a brace tightened one notch too far and left there.\n\nHe sat up.\n\nThe room answered with a soft tilt that corrected itself a heartbeat late. His body was lagging behind his own commands. His limbs moved as if they were accepting delayed instructions. He didn’t fight it. Fighting made mistakes.\n\nHe looked around.\n\nNot the infirmary.\n\nA narrow maintenance room with a cold pipe run along one wall and a low steel table bolted to the floor. Dust in the corners. Chalk scuffs on stone. Someone had used this place for work and then stopped.\n\nHis secondary slate lay on the table. His calibration strap beside it. Three copper-stained chalk pieces laid in a line, tips aligned. Not dumped. Set.\n\nHis primary slate was missing.\n\nHe remembered leaving it behind. The heavy one stayed in the room where he’d been officially “admitted,” because the heavy one would become evidence in someone else’s hands.\n\nHe looked at his palm.\n\nCopper-gold light sat under the skin in thin threads that weren’t veins and weren’t ink. When he flexed, it brightened. When he stopped, it dimmed but didn’t disappear.\n\nMemory came in broken strips.\n\nThe cot. Unable to move. Unable to speak. Breathing and watching and waiting for the body to betray him completely.\n\nA figure in the doorway.\n\nNot shadowed. Not lit. Light fell wrong around it. Edges without behavior.\n\nThe burn in his palm.\n\nThe feeling of something being drawn through him—every nerve, every vessel, every joint—surveyed and set.\n\nHe couldn’t remember a face.\n\nHe couldn’t remember a voice.\n\nHe couldn’t remember why.\n\nOnly the certainty that what happened to him wasn’t a rescue.\n\nIt was a procedure.\n\nA low vibration rolled through the building. Not the normal grid reference-hum. Something heavier, slow enough to make the pipe run above him shiver.\n\nThen came the sound: a deep, muffled bass strike, distant but clean through stone.\n\nHe held his breath. Counted. Listened.\n\nPressure followed. Broad. The table legs squealed against their bolts. Dust dropped in a thin curtain from a seam in the ceiling.\n\nHe stood too fast. The lag punished him; his legs accepted the command late and he had to catch himself on the pipe.\n\nHeat surged behind his sternum. Copper flooded his mouth.\n\nHe tasted brine in it.\n\nHe pulled his kit to him and strapped it on by muscle memory. The buckles clicked. The strap bit his shoulder. Good. Real.\n\nHe wrote one line on the secondary slate:\n\n*A5-T3. Mobility restored. Tremor absent. Phase lag present. Core pressure constant.*\n\nHe tested coupling.\n\nHe focused on the pipe bracket.\n\nThe coupling didn’t start in his hands.\n\nIt started behind his sternum. A tight bloom that pushed outward. Light didn’t spill from his fingers; it bled through his ribs into the metal, into the wall, into the stone underfoot. He felt the stress in the bracket joint without touching it. He felt how close it was to slipping.\n\nHe pushed.\n\nThe bracket settled. The vibration in the pipe smoothed. The structure complied.\n\nHeat surged hard enough to blur his vision at the edges. Clean lines softened into something less reliable.\n\nHe broke coupling fast.\n\nCost, then: heat. Latency. Core-origin coupling. Tremor replaced.\n\nHe wrote:\n\n*Coupling origin shifted (sternum/core). Cost markers: heat surge + visual edge loss + phase lag worsens post-hold.*\n\nWater hit somewhere above him. A slap of brine against stone. Then the sound of glass giving up—one pane, then another—followed by shouting that cut short too quickly.\n\nJhace’s hand clenched on the chalk until the tip snapped.\n\nMobel surfaced in his mind, sharp and immediate.\n\nThe other shard-bearer. The one who held without visible cost markers.\n\nIf anyone had language for integration, it was Mobel.\n\nJhace took what he needed and left by the maintenance corridor the burned report had named—locked, requiring two hands and walking.\n\nHe had both hands.\n\nAnd he could walk.\n\nThat was the only impossible part of today that mattered.\n\n---\n\n# CHAPTER 5: AUTHORITY  \n*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T3*\n\nNews didn’t arrive in the Dregs as proclamations.\n\nIt arrived as pressure.\n\nCorlexi was midway through a termination-threshold recalculation when the building’s vibration changed. The grid reference-hum carried a low shove under its normal band. Stone transmitted it cleanly. Wood transmitted it late.\n\nA messenger came ten minutes later.\n\nNot a Crown courier. Not a Guild runner. A boy with a textile knot tied to his wrist and salt on his sleeves.\n\n“Saint Merro got hit,” the boy said.\n\n“With what?” Corlexi asked.\n\nThe boy’s throat worked. “Sea came into the streets. Doors blew. Windows blew. People dragged out like scrap.”\n\nCorlexi set his pencil down and kept his face neutral.\n\n“How many?” he asked.\n\nThe boy shrugged. “Enough.”\n\nCorlexi slid a coin across the table. The boy took it and vanished.\n\nDisaster did that. It turned “eventual” into “now.”\n\nHe rose and watched the Dregs redistribute load—bundles moved, boards carried, ropes dragged. Salee’s network was visible in the flow.\n\nThen came the other wave.\n\nA Crown-marked wagon rolled into the district with a canvas banner and a soup pot strapped to the back. Behind it walked two clerks with clean cuffs. Behind them walked guards with the posture of enforcement dressed as aid.\n\nThe clerks set a folding table in the street and laid down blank pages like they were laying claim.\n\n“Displaced residents,” the lead clerk called. “Relief allocation requires identification. Name and residence district. Manifestation status if known. Injuries noted. Supplies issued.”\n\nPeople watched from doorways. Nobody cheered.\n\nSoup in exchange for a registry.\n\nCorlexi returned to the table and wrote:\n\n*A5-T3. Saint Merro inundation event. Secondary effects: displacement + uncontrolled manifestation spikes + increased pressure for Crown registry implementation.*\n\nOutside, a manifestor in line tried to hold a buckling awning while also keeping a child from being shoved underfoot. Tremor in both arms. Stage Two pushing into Three. Her hold broke. The awning frame snapped down and clipped a man’s shoulder.\n\nThe crowd surged. Two people’s hands lit with copper-gold filaments in instinctive coupling. The light jumped to the supports, then stuttered. Both manifestors recoiled, pain in their faces, but the awning stayed up long enough for bodies to clear.\n\nUncontrolled coupling. Unmeasured cost. Perfect justification for Crown paperwork.\n\nA guard stepped toward them.\n\nSalee’s network stepped faster.\n\nSomeone shouted “Brace left!” as if that was all the authority required. Someone else shoved a post into place. The crisis moved away from the clerks without ever becoming “an incident” on their pages.\n\nCorlexi received a sealed note before noon.\n\nCrown wax. Simple. Heavy.\n\nHe broke it and read:\n\n**Directive:** accelerate compilation. Prioritize high-output manifestors. Prepare classification categories for “anomalous cost suppression” and “non-consensual stabilization events.” Assign liaisons to relief points.\n\nRelief as a capture mechanism.\n\nHe folded the paper under invoices and wrote where no one would look twice:\n\n*Acceleration acknowledged. Cover risk increased. Salee aware. Relief liaison will tighten scrutiny.*\n\nIf Saint Merro’s ledgers were burning, the Crown would make new ones. It would use the Brinehammer as ink and call it safety.\n\n---\n\n# CHAPTER 6: DREGS  \n*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T5*\n\nSaint Merro didn’t recover. It redistributed damage.\n\nTwo Tides after the Brinehammer, the lower streets still smelled of salt rot and smashed kelp. The harbor quarter looked chewed—doors ripped from hinges, glass ground into mortar, boards jammed into cracks where the sea had punched through.\n\nThe infirmary remained standing. That didn’t mean it was functional.\n\nIt meant it was occupied.\n\nTiffani walked past triage cots lined in the corridor where Jhace had vanished. People lay wrapped in damp blankets with brine-cracked lips and eyes that refused to close. A manifestor shook uncontrollably while a healer held his shoulders down and told him to stop fighting his own nerves.\n\nThe records door was still sealed. Fresh wax over old wax.\n\nAuthority didn’t rebuild. It layered.\n\nBram waited for her at the side exit.\n\n“You’re going to pretend this didn’t change everything?” he asked.\n\n“It changed the environment,” Tiffani said. “Not the methodology.”\n\nBram followed. “That’s the kind of sentence people say right before they die for a principle.”\n\n“Then they die consistent,” Tiffani said.\n\nHe made a sound that wasn’t humor. “You’re insane.”\n\n“I’m correct,” she said.\n\nShe spread her reports. The Brinehammer added failures. Failures added residue. Residue added data.\n\nThe clean-hold signature still led northeast.\n\nGrindheim.\n\nTravel was restricted now. Stabilization protocol. Crown co-mark on Guild authority. Her search permission suspended.\n\nCorran handed her a textile shipment escort pass with Bram’s name on it and didn’t ask her to promise anything. Promises were paperwork too.\n\nThat night they left with a textile wagon crew hauling salvage cloth and salt-stiff rope toward Grindheim. Detours around undermined stone. Temporary bridges held by manifestors whose hands shook too hard to hold their own cups.\n\nBy the time Grindheim’s Dregs rose into view, Tiffani’s knee had locked and released enough times she’d started counting the intervals.\n\nShe still didn’t log it.\n\nThey found Salee by following motion.\n\nInside the unsignposted building, Salee stood over a routing surface—chalk marks, string loops, a glass shard set to throw light sideways.\n\n“You’re the methodist,” Salee said.\n\n“That’s a label,” Tiffani replied.\n\nSalee’s eyes moved over her kit. “Labels tell me where to place you.”\n\n“I’m looking for Jhace Torrins,” Tiffani said. “He vanished under institutional suppression. His trail points here.”\n\nSalee didn’t feign surprise. “Everything points here when the world breaks. Saint Merro got punched. Refugees will come. Authority will follow them. And you walked in with a kit full of order.”\n\n“Order is how people survive,” Tiffani said.\n\n“People survive by adapting,” Salee replied. “Order comes after the body count stops moving.”\n\n“You want my network,” Salee continued. “Witnesses. Routing. Safe passage. The Dregs doesn’t give that away.”\n\n“What do you want,” Tiffani asked.\n\nSalee tapped a copper-stained chalk piece on the table. “Your protocols. How you map cost. How you decide who is about to die. My people hold without permission and pay for it alone. I want a way to predict the payment.”\n\n“Mutual utility,” Tiffani said.\n\n“Terms,” Salee replied. “No faces in your notes. No names unless offered. No routing given to authority.”\n\n“And you give me access,” Tiffani said.\n\nSalee nodded. “Truth as we see it. It comes dirty.”\n\n“Accepted,” Tiffani said.\n\nA runner burst in. “Saint Merro’s sending more. Water took the kiln quarter.”\n\nBram went pale.\n\nTiffani’s throat tightened. The Brinehammer wasn’t finished costing them. It was still writing.\n\nTiffani opened her slate.\n\n“Then we document faster,” she said, because she didn’t have a better weapon.\n\n---\n\n# CHAPTER 7: TRACKING  \n*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T6*\n\nThe coast smelled wrong—brine, smashed weed, rot lifted from places it didn’t belong.\n\nJhace walked north with his kit tight against his ribs. Phase lag made every step slightly mistimed. He corrected by counting.\n\nHe tracked Mobel by wake: dock pilings braced too clean, warehouse walls stabilized mid-fail with residue concentrated at joints, stone stairs corrected without the smear patterns tremoring hands left behind.\n\nNow saturation made stress points everywhere, and the shard behind his sternum answered each one.\n\nHe refused three holds before noon and paid for refusal anyway. Heat built. Phase lag worsened. His hands stayed steady while his body felt less his.\n\nAt a coastal settlement, a pier crane leaned toward collapse. A soaked man stood under it and stared up.\n\nJhace walked past.\n\nTen steps later the shard surged without permission.\n\nLight pressed from his sternum into stone. The crane joint locked. The lean stopped.\n\nJhace cut coupling and kept moving. Heat hit hard. Copper flooded his mouth. A thin smear of blood appeared on his wrist when he wiped.\n\nHis palm glowed brighter. The copper-gold threads climbed another finger’s width toward his wrist.\n\nHe wrote while walking:\n\n*A5-T6. Involuntary hold triggered by proximity + saturation stress. Cost: heat spike + oral bleed + spread accelerated.*\n\nWitnesses spoke about “the calm one” who fixed a pier brace and didn’t shake afterward. Mobel. Three Tides ago, maybe two.\n\nJhace didn’t ask too many questions. Faces made him trackable.\n\nRefugees moved inland along the road, carts rattling, bundles soaked. One woman saw the glow under his sleeve and backed away like he was an exposed edge.\n\nJhace pulled his sleeve down and kept walking.\n\nMobel’s wake led toward higher ground—fresh residue at a culvert, clean joint locks on a bridge support undermined by floodwater.\n\nJhace followed.\n\nHis chest pressure didn’t ease.\n\nIf anything, it felt pleased.\n\n---\n\n# CHAPTER 8: ENTROPY  \n*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T7*\n\nVelocity-Heights sat above the Dregs on newer stone and older arrogance.\n\nIt took Brinehammer pressure as bodies at the gates, not water in the streets. Refugees were being funneled into glass-roofed halls and “temporary” shelters built from lattice and pane. The architecture looked precise. The loads were not.\n\nCorlexi came alone under cover: merchant verification, relief-routing assessment.\n\nReal purpose: Eries.\n\nHe found her in a public garden where glass lattice broke sunlight into hard lines across stone. Students sat around her with open slates.\n\n“The Brinehammer isn’t a moral event,” Eries said. “It isn’t punishment. It is force meeting structure. If your structure lies to itself, force will correct the lie.”\n\nWhen the students dispersed, Eries didn’t turn to Corlexi immediately.\n\n“You’re not here for shipping,” she said.\n\n“Crown?” she added, and didn’t wait for him to answer.\n\n“You write about decay,” Corlexi said.\n\n“I write about reality,” Eries replied. “People want someone to blame because blame feels like control.”\n\n“The registry prevents uncontrolled risk.”\n\n“The registry labels risk,” Eries said. “After it’s already moving.”\n\nA shout rose beyond the garden wall. A relief crew dragged glass panes toward a shelter hall. The panes were strapped wrong. The load shifted. The top sheet flexed and began to bow.\n\nA crack sang out—thin, sharp—and the sheet started to split.\n\nPeople scattered. Some didn’t move fast enough.\n\nEries stepped into the churn and ordered, “Down,” and the nearest students dropped without arguing.\n\nCorlexi saw the stress line forming. The glass wasn’t annealed for this transport. It was meant to sit, not be hauled through panic.\n\nHe could correct it with Form.\n\nHe also knew Crown guards were watching.\n\nHe moved anyway.\n\nHe put his hands on the wooden brace like any other man steadying a load. Under that physical exertion, he let Form slide into strap path and brace angle.\n\nA slight correction. A load shift. The strain moved out of the crack line.\n\nThe glass stopped singing. The split halted on a hairline.\n\nA guard stared directly at Corlexi.\n\nEries watched him too, not surprised—confirming.\n\nWhen the crisis passed, Eries returned to the path and wiped glass dust from her palm.\n\n“You intervened,” she said.\n\n“I prevented waste.”\n\n“You prevented blood,” Eries corrected. “Without visible cost markers.”\n\nCorlexi held his voice flat. “Observation is a habit of yours.”\n\n“Reality is.” Eries looked toward the city below. “Your registry will not stop holds from failing. It will decide who gets blamed when they do.”\n\n“What’s the alternative,” Corlexi asked.\n\n“Admit you don’t control failure,” Eries said. “You manage it. You choose where it lands. If you pretend you aren’t choosing, you’ll still choose—blindly.”\n\nCorlexi left the Heights with his cover intact and a new problem: Eries now had proof of what he could do.\n\nKnowledge spread on its own schedule.\n\n---\n\n# CHAPTER 9: COLD  \n*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T8*\n\nThe Dregs didn’t keep trails the way stone kept scuffs.\n\nSalee’s network gave Tiffani what it could: three sightings in two Tides. A man moving wrong—step landing late, body correcting after. Hands steady when they shouldn’t be. A copper-gold glow glimpsed at a sleeve edge. Always northeast.\n\nTiffani mapped each point and watched the geometry degrade.\n\nNot because the pattern was wrong.\n\nBecause the pattern stopped being physical.\n\nIt became behavioral.\n\nAt the Dregs edge, reliable sighting dissolved into “maybe” and “don’t ask me again.”\n\nBram stood beside her under a bent awning. “He’s not here, Tiff.”\n\n“The data doesn’t support that.”\n\n“The data doesn’t support anything,” Bram snapped, then softened. “People lie here to stay alive.”\n\nSalee approached without announcement. “You’ve reached the end of what physics can prove,” she said.\n\n“Physics doesn’t end.”\n\n“Your access ends,” Salee replied. “Your assumptions end.”\n\nTiffani kept her chalk steady. “He chose concealment.”\n\n“Or he chose not to be your experiment,” Salee said.\n\nBram’s gaze stayed on Tiffani’s hands. “If he doesn’t want to be found, you’re tracking a decision.”\n\nTiffani wrote:\n\n*Trail cold. Physical markers absent. Variable introduced: will.*\n\nThe line felt like admitting defeat.\n\nThen she wrote:\n\n*Redirect objective: Mobel.*\n\n“That’s smarter,” Salee said.\n\n“More measurable,” Tiffani replied.\n\nBram looked tired. “Still not logging your own cost.”\n\nTiffani’s chalk snapped. She stared at the broken tip and put it away.\n\n“I’m fine,” she said.\n\n“You’re lying,” Bram said. “I see.”\n\nTiffani didn’t answer.\n\nThat night she wrote one sentence in a private margin:\n\n*My body is becoming data I refuse to record.*\n\nHer hand shook while she wrote it.\n\nShe still didn’t move it into the official log.\n\n---\n\n# CHAPTER 10: INTERSECTION  \n*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T9*\n\nThe settlement had a lighthouse stump and a main street lined with broken glass from the Brinehammer’s edge.\n\nJhace arrived and felt stress before he saw it: a warehouse frame pulled out of square. Wet load in the roof. One support joint already eating itself under shifting weight.\n\nThe shard tightened.\n\nHe told it no.\n\nIt surged anyway.\n\nLight pressed outward through him and into stone. The warehouse frame settled. The joint locked long enough for people inside to get out.\n\nJhace cut coupling and staggered one step as phase lag hit.\n\nHeat slammed his ribs. Copper flooded his mouth. Blood smeared thin on his wrist when he wiped.\n\nHe kept moving.\n\n“You’re the other one.”\n\nJhace turned.\n\nA woman in practical clothes stood in a broken doorway’s lee. No tremor. A tool on her belt—glass, wire, shard sliver—hummed faintly.\n\n“Define ‘other,’” Jhace said.\n\n“Internal integration. Like Mobel.” She stepped closer. “Name’s Vael. I repair what can be repaired. I watch what can’t.”\n\n“You’re watching me.”\n\n“I’m measuring your wake,” Vael said. “You’re leaving clean locks half the time without meaning to.”\n\n“I need answers. Mobel will know.”\n\n“Mobel’s been running for three Anneals. He doesn’t share.” Vael’s mouth tightened. “Because being a solution makes you a target.”\n\n“I’m being hunted,” Jhace said.\n\nVael nodded once. “Yes.”\n\n“How close.”\n\n“Two Tides behind you. Maybe less now.” Vael’s eyes stayed steady. “They tidy. They overwrite residue. They ask questions after you leave and make people forget they answered.”\n\nJhace wrote quickly:\n\n*A5-T9. Vael confirmed pursuit. Pursuer performs residue correction + witness management. External shard-tool exists; costs differ, not absent.*\n\n“What do you want,” he asked.\n\n“I want you to decide whether catching Mobel is worth being caught,” Vael said. “Because your wake is loud, and the Brinehammer made stress points everywhere. You’re trackable.”\n\nShe stepped back toward shadow.\n\n“Why tell me,” Jhace asked.\n\n“Because I’ve seen what happens when someone becomes infrastructure,” Vael said. “People stop seeing you as a person.”\n\nThen she was gone.\n\nJhace stood with copper in his mouth and pressure in his chest and the road ahead leading to Mobel.\n\nHe chose forward.\n\n---\n\n# CHAPTER 11: COERCION  \n*(POV: Corlexi) | A5-T10*\n\nCorlexi returned to his workspace and knew it had been read.\n\nDust patterns wrong. Ledger edge misaligned. One pin rotated slightly.\n\nUnder his pillow: a copper-stained chalk piece with Vael’s maker notch.\n\nA message without ink.\n\nHe pocketed it and left.\n\nThe Dregs had changed again. Refugees arrived steady—salt-cracked skin, damp bundles, eyes that scanned upward.\n\nThe Crown relief table had grown. Two clerks wrote faster. A guard stood closer.\n\n“Name and district,” the clerk called.\n\nA starving man gave a name and got soup.\n\nThen the guard asked quietly, close: “Who else manifested during the inundation.”\n\nThat wasn’t relief.\n\nThat was harvesting.\n\nVael stood near a broken fountain, watching flow like a structural inspector.\n\n“You searched my workspace,” Corlexi said.\n\n“I confirmed a suspicion,” Vael replied. “Crown embedded. Registry architect.”\n\n“And you’re an independent operator with external coupling technology.”\n\n“Accurate.”\n\n“You could expose me.”\n\n“I could,” Vael said. “Exposure isn’t my goal.”\n\n“State your goal.”\n\n“Your registry is going to use the Brinehammer as justification,” Vael said. “Soup for names.”\n\n“Documentation enables allocation,” Corlexi said.\n\n“Documentation enables control,” Vael shot back. “And control chooses who gets protected. You’ve already started choosing.”\n\nA shout rose near the relief table. A woman refused to give a name. A guard stepped forward.\n\nSalee’s network moved—boards “accidentally” blocked the guard, soup spilled, attention snapped to cleanup. The woman vanished into the crowd.\n\nSalee passed nearby and didn’t look at either of them.\n\nCorlexi placed the copper-stained chalk on the fountain edge between them. “Then give me data that matters. Locations. signatures. Timings.”\n\nVael stared at the chalk. “You’re bargaining.”\n\n“I’m always bargaining,” Corlexi said.\n\nVael pocketed the chalk. “Fine. The pursuer behind the integrated one—Jhace. They don’t move like Crown. Their corrections aren’t bureaucratic. They are precise.”\n\nCold settled behind Corlexi’s ribs.\n\nWood snapped nearby. A shelter wall brace bowed under wet weight and too many bodies.\n\nTwo manifestors flared filaments at the same time. Their coupling overlapped, fought, stuttered. One screamed and fell back clutching his forearm. The other held five heartbeats and dropped.\n\nThe wall started to go.\n\nCorlexi stepped in with a plank like any other man bracing wood. He wedged it under the failing joint and pushed with his shoulder.\n\nUnder that physical effort, he let Form slide into the brace’s stress line.\n\nJust enough.\n\nThe brace stopped bowing. The wall steadied. Hands stacked a second support and lashed it tight.\n\nNo visible light.\n\nBut the shift was too clean.\n\nVael watched him without blinking. Somewhere in the crowd, Salee watched too.\n\nCorlexi backed out as soon as the secondary brace took weight. He didn’t wait for thanks.\n\nBack in his workspace he wrote under grain invoices:\n\n*A5-T10. Unknown pursuer active: residue correction + witness shaping. Threat classification: non-institutional, precise. Relief registry escalating under disaster cover. Cover strain increased via emergency intervention.*\n\nFor the first time since the Piercing, the enemy in his notes didn’t have a stamp.\n\n---\n\n# CHAPTER 12: LIGHT CRACKS  \n*(POV: Jhace Torrins) | A5-T11*\n\nThe inland road rose into harder ground.\n\nLess brine. Less rot. More stone that hadn’t been soaked and hollowed.\n\nIt didn’t feel safer.\n\nMobel’s wake was fresh—one Tide ahead, maybe less. Culvert brace locked too clean. Bridge support joint sealed with copper-gold geometry. Barn frame corrected mid-sag with no witness reporting tremor afterward.\n\nJhace pushed.\n\nPhase lag was worse. Each step landed late. Each correction arrived after the mistake.\n\nThe road crossed a bridge where floodwater had scoured the base of the supports.\n\nThe bridge groaned under his boots.\n\nJhace kept walking.\n\nThe shard surged anyway.\n\nLight pressed out from his sternum into stone and iron. The joint locked. The groan stopped. The bridge held.\n\nHeat slammed through him.\n\nNot just heat. A sensation of tissue shifting, quiet and relentless.\n\nHe dropped to one knee on the deck.\n\nCopper filled his mouth. Blood came with it, thicker now.\n\nHe looked at his arm.\n\nThe copper-gold threads had climbed past his wrist. They sat under skin in a lattice that didn’t match veins. When his heart beat, the glow answered.\n\nHe wrote on his slate:\n\n*A5-T11. Involuntary hold. Spread advanced beyond wrist. Heat spike severe. Oral bleed recurring. Phase lag worsening. Termination threshold unknown.*\n\nHe forced himself upright.\n\nThe road behind him was empty.\n\nHe didn’t trust that.\n\nA faint sound behind him—stone against stone, careful.\n\nJhace stopped and listened.\n\nNothing.\n\nThen a suppressed cough, human and close enough to mean patience.\n\nThe pursuer wasn’t a rumor anymore.\n\nJhace didn’t turn.\n\nHe kept moving because Mobel was still ahead, still leaving that clean wake, still holding answers Jhace didn’t have.\n\nThree-way chase:\n\nJhace pursuing Mobel.  \nThe pursuer pursuing Jhace.  \nThe shard pursuing failure points whether he consented or not.\n\nThe Brinehammer rewrote Saint Merro with water and force.\n\nWhatever rewrote Jhace was doing the same thing to his body—quiet, structural, invasive.\n\nLight cracked through him with every step.\n\nHe didn’t know the final cost.\n\nHe kept walking anyway.\n\n---\n\"\"\"\n\nout_path = Path(\"/mnt/data/N02_LIGHT_CRACKS_THROUGH_FULL_DRAFT.md\")\nout_path.write_text(text, encoding=\"utf-8\")\n\nwc = len(text.split())\n(out_path.as_posix(), wc)\n``"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1767954988.153104,
        "end_turn": false,
        "id": "ba078e82-78f5-4406-a13e-4e76a596a273",
        "metadata": {
          "can_save": false,

              200012
            ],

          "parent_id": "ba9ae301-155e-44a0-ab5e-89f150346c45",

          "turn_exchange_id": "e0e1dc59-a397-4186-925c-f19f89f77346"

        "recipient": "python",

    },
    "ba544fa6-9fb6-4687-8cf7-f52e0994c585": {
      "children": [
        "af2e46e7-449a-424f-b84a-5d36281dd7b8"
      ],
      "id": "ba544fa6-9fb6-4687-8cf7-f52e0994c585",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "On the table: grain tallies, transport costs, dull merchant arguments that made a man forget he was being watched.\n\nOn the walls—when uncovered—incident maps, cost progression curves, termination thresholds, name lists compiled without consent because consent slowed control.\n\nA knock came at the wrong rhythm.\n\nHe moved without haste. Charts slid under a ledger. Shoulders softened into merchant posture.\n\nAn older woman stood in the doorway. Textile calluses. Eyes that didn’t waste motion.\n\n“Message from Salee,” she said. “She says you’re documenting the Dregs.”\n\n“I document inventory,” Corlexi replied. “Documentation keeps business from bleeding.”\n\n“Salee says documentation *is* business.” The woman’s gaze stayed on his face. “She wanted you to know she sees you.”\n\nShe left.\n\nCorlexi waited until her footfall faded. Then he unbuttoned his shirt to the waist.\n\nScars cut diagonal across his torso—pale seams where copper-gold fused into tissue during the Piercing. Twelve fragments, each no larger than a thumbnail. Counted enough times to become reflex.\n\nHe tested.\n\nNo tremor. No exhaustion drift. No vein-lines creeping up his forearms.\n\nHe focused on a crooked lamp bracket across the room. Read it: iron. stress at the joint. fasteners failing by degrees for at least two Anneals.\n\nHe adjusted the structure.\n\nThe bracket settled with a soft click and held as if it had never been wrong.\n\nForm. Structure maintained.\n\nNo filament light. No copper spill. No visible cost marker.\n\nThat absence made him valuable.\n\nIt also made him an anomaly that would eventually be “resolved.”\n\nHe buttoned up and opened the collapse reports.\n\nSeven incidents in three Tides. Saint Merro held most of them—still bleeding, still pretending it had regained baseline. The holds were too clean. Casualty projections weren’t matching. Residue was concentrating in ways standard dispersal didn’t produce.\n\nSomeone else was operating outside the visible cost curve.\n\nHe wrote, not as speculation, but as risk management:\n\n*Internal shard integration may suppress standard cost markers. Confirm. Identify additional subjects.*\n\nA low vibration rolled through the wall—slow enough to register as a change, not background. He didn’t look at the window. Looking was a tell.\n\nThe corridor outside filled with directed foot traffic. Not panic. Routing. Networks.\n\nA second knock. Harder.\n\nHe opened the door to a runner with salt on his cuffs and a textile knot tied to his wrist.\n\n“Saint Merro got struck,” the runner said. “Water in the streets. People moving. Refugees coming.”\n\nCorlexi kept his face dull. “Why tell me.”\n\n“Because you write,” the runner said. “Writing turns into planning. Planning keeps floors from dropping.”\n\nA folded list went into Corlexi’s hand. Not names. Needs: rope, boards, dry cloth, nails, bread, wedges. Door bracing. Makeshift splinting. Clean water.\n\nHe felt, briefly, the registry become useful the way a prybar was useful. Not moral. Effective.\n\nHe slid the list under grain totals. “Where’s Salee.”\n\n“Routing the first wave. Also—your building’s taking load.”\n\n“Load.”\n\n“Bodies,” the runner snapped. “Too many. Too fast. Floors weren’t built for it.”\n\nRunner gone.\n\nCorlexi listened to his building. Stone carried weight clean. Wood carried it late. The disagreement had been there for months. Now it mattered.\n\nHe went downstairs.\n\nResidents were already packing. Not a formal shelter. Just a place people believed would still have a roof when patched structures elsewhere failed.\n\nA woman with brine-stiff hair shoved a bundle under a table. A man lifted a child onto a bench. Someone slammed a door; the frame answered with a complaint.\n\nCorlexi saw the central beam: hairline split at a join, old damage widened by sudden shifting load. It had an hour. Maybe two.\n\nIf it dropped, people would surge. Surge turned into uncontrolled coupling. Uncontrolled coupling turned into bodies.\n\nHe could intervene.\n\nHe could be seen.\n\nSalee’s people were here. He recognized them: eyes tracking exits, hands brushing beams without looking like they were measuring.\n\nHe moved like a merchant checking stock. Set down a crate, leaned, and slid focus into the split join.\n\nForm. Quiet. Precise.\n\nHe didn’t “fix” it. He redistributed load enough to buy time. The split stopped widening. Vibration steadied.\n\nCost: none visible.\n\nRisk: immediate.\n\nA man near the wall turned his head sharply, as if he’d felt something without knowing what. The runner’s eyes flicked to Corlexi’s hands.\n\nCorlexi straightened, picked up a sack of grain, and performed irritation.\n\n“If you’re stacking people, stack them against stone, not mid-span. You want the floor to stay a floor.”\n\nIt sounded like merchant complaint.\n\nIt was also correct.\n\nPeople shifted. Load redistributed by human choice. That mattered. It gave his adjustment a mundane cause.\n\nUpstairs, he wrote one line on a merchant sheet:\n\n*Brinehammer displacement begun. Dregs taking load. Structural risk increased. Cover risk increased.*\n\nThen, smaller:\n\n*Salee sees me. Now her network may have felt me.*\n\nHe folded the page under grain totals and resumed the posture of a man who cared only about prices.\n\n---\n\n## CHAPTER 3: WAKE\n\n*(POV: Tiffani Merrow) | A5-T3 through A5-T7*\n\nSeven reports. Seven incidents. Three Tides.\n\nTiffani laid them across her workspace and refused long blinks. Patterns showed themselves when you didn’t flinch.\n\nSeam collapse near Junction Twelve boundary. Estimated hold duration: two hundred heartbeats. Zero casualties where her structural projection demanded four minimum. Copper-gold residue concentrated at load junctions instead of dispersing into surrounding material.\n\nFoundation stabilization at Merchant Row—same residue behavior.\n\nBridge support failure at the southern canal—again.\n\nEach site carried a mode surge in the grid reference-hum that didn’t match ambient drift. Not noise. A signature.\n\nShe plotted locations on a city map and drew lines with calibrated tools. The geometry held.\n\nThe vector pointed northeast.\n\nGrindheim.\n\nA quiet scrape at her door. Not a knock. Someone trying not to exist in the corridor’s memory.\n\nCorran stepped in and shut it behind him. Mid-level Guild administration. Tired eyes. Restricted stamp on the folder in his hand. A man walking into a decision he already hated.\n\n“Unofficially,” he said, “you did not get this from me.”\n\nHe set the folder down. Didn’t sit.\n\n“The director is calling it clerical loss,” Corran continued. “Lost ledger pages. Misfiled routing. Your search authorization gets revoked next Tide.”\n\nTiffani moved on instinct to the archive cabinet. Pulled the Saint Merro incident drawer.\n\nIt didn’t open.\n\nNot stuck—locked. Fresh metal. New keyway.\n\nCorran stared at the floor.\n\n“They moved fast,” Tiffani said.\n\n“They moved before I could stop them,” Corran replied.\n\nShe rested her fingers on the new lock long enough to feel its cold, then stepped back.\n\n“Good,” she said. “Now it’s measurable.”\n\nCorran’s throat worked. “Someone doesn’t want you following this.”\n\nShe opened the folder.\n\nEighth incident. Grindheim Dregs. Old textile district. Same residue concentration. Same hold cleanliness. Same missing casualties.\n\n“Why help me,” she asked.\n\n“Because your ledger page wasn’t lost,” Corran said, low. “It was removed. I saw the request in archive before it got scrubbed. Administrative channel. Crown-level reach.”\n\nObstruction: confirmed.\n\nInterference: funded.\n\nTiffani kept her face still. “What do you want.”\n\n“When you understand what happened—when you learn what was done to him—send word outside the official channels.” Corran didn’t offer method. “Don’t send it where they can erase it.”\n\nHe left without waiting for agreement.\n\nTwo Tides later, she found Bram in the Guild hall with a chipped cup and something dark inside it.\n\n“I need access to Grindheim,” she said. “Before revocation lands. You have textile ties and harbor adjacency. Get me a reason that opens doors.”\n"
        },
        "create_time": 1767959321.2200928,
        "end_turn": null,
